title: Brown University

infobox.value: * [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [COFHE](/wiki/Consortium_on_Financing_Higher_Education "Consortium on Financing Higher Education") * [URA](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") * [NAICU](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities") * [Space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")
[Francis J. Doyle III](/wiki/Francis_J._Doyle_III "Francis J. Doyle III")

[Providence, Rhode Island](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island") , United States  [41°49′34″N 71°24′11″W﻿ / ﻿41.826°N 71.403°W﻿ / 41.826; -71.403](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Brown_University&params=41.826_N_71.403_W_region:US-RI_type:edu)
*[The Brown Daily Herald](/wiki/The_Brown_Daily_Herald "The Brown Daily Herald")*
Seal brown, cardinal red, and white[[7]](#cite_note-7)

* [NCAA Division I FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_FCS "NCAA Division I FCS") – [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") * [ECAC Hockey](/wiki/ECAC_Hockey "ECAC Hockey") * [EARC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges") * [EAWRC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Women%27s_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Women's Rowing Colleges") * [NEISA](/wiki/New_England_Intercollegiate_Sailing_Association "New England Intercollegiate Sailing Association") * [CWPA](/wiki/Collegiate_Water_Polo_Association "Collegiate Water Polo Association") * [IRA](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Rowing_Association "Intercollegiate Rowing Association")

is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [College Hill](/wiki/College_Hill,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "College Hill, Providence, Rhode Island") neighborhood of [Providence, Rhode Island](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island"), United States. The university is the [seventh-oldest institution](/wiki/List_of_colonial_colleges "List of colonial colleges") of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in the United States, founded in 1764 as the *College in the English Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations*. One of nine [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) it was the first American college to codify that admission and instruction of students was to be equal regardless of the [religious affiliation](/wiki/Religious_affiliation "Religious affiliation") of students.[[9]](#cite_note-:7.30-9)

**Brown University** is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [College Hill](/wiki/College_Hill,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "College Hill, Providence, Rhode Island") neighborhood of [Providence, Rhode Island](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island"), United States. The university is the [seventh-oldest institution](/wiki/List_of_colonial_colleges "List of colonial colleges") of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education") in the United States, founded in 1764 as the *College in the English Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations*. One of nine [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") chartered before the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) it was the first American college to codify that admission and instruction of students was to be equal regardless of the [religious affiliation](/wiki/Religious_affiliation "Religious affiliation") of students.[[9]](#cite_note-:7.30-9)

The university is home to the oldest [applied mathematics](/wiki/Applied_mathematics "Applied mathematics") program in the country and oldest engineering program in the Ivy League.[[a]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-11)[[11]](#cite_note-:14b-12)[[12]](#cite_note-:13-13) It was one of the early doctoral-granting institutions in the U.S., adding masters and doctoral studies in 1887.[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14) In 1969, it adopted its [Open Curriculum](/wiki/Open_Curriculum_(Brown_University) "Open Curriculum (Brown University)") after student lobbying, which eliminated mandatory [general education](/wiki/Curriculum#Core_curriculum "Curriculum") distribution requirements.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) In 1971, Brown's coordinate women's institution, [Pembroke College](/wiki/Pembroke_College_in_Brown_University "Pembroke College in Brown University"), was fully merged into the university.

The university comprises the [College](/wiki/College_of_Brown_University "College of Brown University"), the [Graduate School](/wiki/Graduate_School_of_Brown_University "Graduate School of Brown University"), [Alpert Medical School](/wiki/Alpert_Medical_School "Alpert Medical School"), the [School of Engineering](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Engineering "Brown University School of Engineering"), the [School of Public Health](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Public_Health "Brown University School of Public Health") and the School of Professional Studies. Its international programs are organized through the [Watson School of International and Public Affairs](/wiki/Watson_School_of_International_and_Public_Affairs "Watson School of International and Public Affairs"), and it is academically affiliated with the [Marine Biological Laboratory](/wiki/Marine_Biological_Laboratory "Marine Biological Laboratory") and the [Rhode Island School of Design](/wiki/Rhode_Island_School_of_Design "Rhode Island School of Design"), which offers undergraduate and graduate [dual degree programs](#Brown_and_RISD). The university is surrounded by a [federally listed architectural district](/wiki/College_Hill_Historic_District_(Providence,_Rhode_Island) "College Hill Historic District (Providence, Rhode Island)") with a concentration of [colonial-era](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States") buildings. [Benefit Street](/wiki/Benefit_Street "Benefit Street") has one of America's richest concentrations of 17th- and 18th-century architecture.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) Undergraduate admissions are among the most selective in the country, with an acceptance rate of 5% for the class of 2026.[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)

As of October 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit), 12 [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") winners, 1 [Fields Medalist](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), 7 [National Humanities Medalists](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal"),[[b]](#cite_note-21) and 11 [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") laureates have been affiliated with Brown as [alumni](/wiki/List_of_Brown_University_alumni "List of Brown University alumni"), [faculty, or researchers](/wiki/List_of_Brown_University_faculty "List of Brown University faculty"). Alumni also include 29 [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners,[[c]](#cite_note-23) 21 billionaires,[[d]](#cite_note-29) 4 [U.S. secretaries of state](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State"), over 100 members of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"),[[26]](#cite_note-30) 58 [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"),[[27]](#cite_note-31) 22 [MacArthur Genius](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program") Fellows,[[e]](#cite_note-32) and 38 Olympic medalists.[[28]](#cite_note-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Brown University](/wiki/History_of_Brown_University "History of Brown University")

### Foundation and charter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation and charter")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Samuel_King_-_Ezra_Stiles_%281727_-1795%29%2C_B.A._1746%2C_M.A._1749_-_1955.3.1_-_Yale_University_Art_Gallery.jpg/250px-Samuel_King_-_Ezra_Stiles_%281727_-1795%29%2C_B.A._1746%2C_M.A._1749_-_1955.3.1_-_Yale_University_Art_Gallery.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samuel_King_-_Ezra_Stiles_(1727_-1795),_B.A._1746,_M.A._1749_-_1955.3.1_-_Yale_University_Art_Gallery.jpg)

Petitioner [Ezra Stiles](/wiki/Ezra_Stiles "Ezra Stiles") later became the [seventh president](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Yale_University "List of presidents of Yale University") of [Yale College](/wiki/Yale_College "Yale College").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/William_Ellery_%28H._B._Hall%29.jpg/250px-William_Ellery_%28H._B._Hall%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Ellery_(H._B._Hall).jpg)

Petitioner [William Ellery](/wiki/William_Ellery "William Ellery") signed the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") in 1776.

In 1761, three residents of [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island"), drafted a petition to the colony's [General Assembly](/wiki/Rhode_Island_General_Assembly "Rhode Island General Assembly"):[[29]](#cite_note-:8-34)

> That your Petitioners propose to open a literary institution or School for instructing young Gentlemen in the Languages, Mathematics, Geography & History, & such other branches of Knowledge as shall be desired. That for this End ... it will be necessary ... to erect a public Building or Buildings for the boarding of the youth & the Residence of the Professors.

The three petitioners were [Ezra Stiles](/wiki/Ezra_Stiles "Ezra Stiles"), pastor of Newport's [Second Congregational Church](/wiki/Clarke_Street_Meeting_House "Clarke Street Meeting House") and future president of [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"); [William Ellery Jr.](/wiki/William_Ellery "William Ellery"), future [signer](/wiki/Signing_of_the_United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "Signing of the United States Declaration of Independence") of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"); and [Josias Lyndon](/wiki/Josias_Lyndon "Josias Lyndon"), future governor of the colony. Stiles and Ellery later served as co-authors of the college's charter two years later. The editor of Stiles's papers observes, "This draft of a petition connects itself with other evidence of Dr. Stiles's project for a Collegiate Institution in Rhode Island, before the charter of what became Brown University."[[29]](#cite_note-:8-34)[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

The [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") Baptist Association was also interested in establishing a college in Rhode Island, which was founded as a [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") colony in America, including the mother-church of all [American Baptists](/wiki/American_Baptists "American Baptists"): the [First Baptist Church in America](/wiki/First_Baptist_Church_in_America "First Baptist Church in America"). At the time, the [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") were unrepresented among the colonial colleges; the [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States") had [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") and Yale University, the [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") had the College of New Jersey, which later became [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"), and the [Episcopalians](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") had the [College of William & Mary](/wiki/College_of_William_%26_Mary "College of William & Mary") and King's College, which later became [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"). The local [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") in their native Philadelphia was founded by [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") without direct association with any particular denomination.[[31]](#cite_note-36) [Isaac Backus](/wiki/Isaac_Backus "Isaac Backus"), the Baptist minister and historian of the New England Baptists, who was an inaugural trustee of Brown University, wrote of the October 1762 resolution taken at Philadelphia:[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

> The Philadelphia Association obtained such an acquaintance with our affairs, as to bring them to an apprehension that it was practicable and expedient to erect a college in the Colony of Rhode-Island, under the chief direction of the Baptists; ... Mr. [James Manning](/wiki/James_Manning_(minister) "James Manning (minister)"), who took his first degree in [New-Jersey college](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") in September, 1762, was esteemed a suitable leader in this important work.

James Manning arrived at Newport in July 1763 and was introduced to Stiles, who agreed to write the charter for the college. Stiles' first draft was read to the General Assembly in August 1763, and rejected by Baptist members who worried that their denomination would be underrepresented in the College Board of Fellows. A revised charter written by Stiles and Ellery was adopted by the Rhode Island General Assembly on March 3, 1764, in [East Greenwich](/wiki/East_Greenwich,_Rhode_Island "East Greenwich, Rhode Island").[[32]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/James_Manning_head_by_Cosmo_Alexander.jpg/250px-James_Manning_head_by_Cosmo_Alexander.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Manning_head_by_Cosmo_Alexander.jpg)

Brown's first president, minister [James Manning](/wiki/James_Manning_(minister) "James Manning (minister)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Brown_University_Ezra_Stiles_Charter.jpg/250px-Brown_University_Ezra_Stiles_Charter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University_Ezra_Stiles_Charter.jpg)

The [Ezra Stiles](/wiki/Ezra_Stiles "Ezra Stiles") copy of Brown's 1764 charter

In September 1764, the inaugural meeting of the corporation—the college's governing body—was held in Newport's [Old Colony House](/wiki/Old_Colony_House "Old Colony House"). Governor [Stephen Hopkins](/wiki/Stephen_Hopkins_(politician) "Stephen Hopkins (politician)") was chosen chancellor, former and future governor [Samuel Ward](/wiki/Samuel_Ward_(Rhode_Island_politician) "Samuel Ward (Rhode Island politician)") vice chancellor, John Tillinghast treasurer, and Thomas Eyres secretary. The charter stipulated that the board of trustees should be composed of 22 Baptists, 5 [Quakers](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends"), 5 Episcopalians, and 4 Congregationalists. Of the 12 Fellows, 8 should be Baptists—including the college president—"and the rest indifferently of any or all Denominations."[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

At the time of its creation, Brown's charter was a uniquely progressive document.[[33]](#cite_note-38) Other colleges had curricular strictures against opposing doctrines, while Brown's charter asserted, "Sectarian differences of opinions, shall not make any Part of the Public and Classical Instruction." The document additionally "recognized more broadly and fundamentally than any other [university charter] the principle of denominational cooperation."[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14) The oft-repeated statement that Brown's charter alone prohibited a religious test for College membership is inaccurate; other college charters were similarly liberal in that particular.[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Brown_University_1792_engraving.jpg/250px-Brown_University_1792_engraving.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University_1792_engraving.jpg)

This 1792 engraving is the first published image of Brown. [University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall_(Brown_University) "University Hall (Brown University)") stands on the right while the President's House sits on the left.

The college was founded as Rhode Island College, at the site of the First Baptist Church in Warren, Rhode Island.[[35]](#cite_note-Beebe-40) Manning was sworn in as the college's first president in 1765 and remained in the role until 1791. In 1766, the college authorized [Morgan Edwards](/wiki/Morgan_Edwards "Morgan Edwards") to travel to Europe to "solicit Benefactions for this Institution".[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39) During his year-and-a-half stay in the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles_naming_dispute "British Isles naming dispute"), Edwards secured funding from benefactors including [Thomas Penn](/wiki/Thomas_Penn "Thomas Penn") and [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin").[[34]](#cite_note-:4-39)

In 1770, the college moved from Warren to Providence. To establish a campus, [John](/wiki/John_Brown_(Rhode_Island_politician) "John Brown (Rhode Island politician)") and [Moses Brown](/wiki/Moses_Brown "Moses Brown") purchased a four-acre lot on the crest of [College Hill](/wiki/College_Hill,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "College Hill, Providence, Rhode Island") on behalf of the school. The majority of the property fell within the bounds of the original home lot of [Chad Brown](/wiki/Chad_Brown_(minister) "Chad Brown (minister)"), an ancestor of the Browns and one of the original proprietors of [Providence Plantations](/wiki/Providence_Plantations "Providence Plantations").[[36]](#cite_note-41) After the college was relocated to the city, work began on constructing its first building.

A building committee, organized by the corporation, developed plans for the college's first purpose-built edifice, finalizing a design on February 9, 1770. The subsequent structure, referred to as "The College Edifice" and later as [University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall_(Brown_University) "University Hall (Brown University)"), may have been modeled on [Nassau Hall](/wiki/Nassau_Hall "Nassau Hall"), built 14 years prior at the [College of New Jersey](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"). President Manning, an active member of the building process, was educated at Princeton and might have suggested that Brown's first building resemble that of his [alma mater](/wiki/Alma_mater "Alma mater").[[37]](#cite_note-:03-42)

#### Brown family

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Brown family")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Nicholas_Brown_Jr_by_Chester_Harding_1836.jpg/250px-Nicholas_Brown_Jr_by_Chester_Harding_1836.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicholas_Brown_Jr_by_Chester_Harding_1836.jpg)

Following the gift of [Nicholas Brown Jr.](/wiki/Nicholas_Brown_Jr. "Nicholas Brown Jr.") (Class of 1786), the university was renamed in his honor.

[Nicholas Brown](/wiki/Nicholas_Brown_Sr. "Nicholas Brown Sr."), [John Brown](/wiki/John_Brown_(Rhode_Island_politician) "John Brown (Rhode Island politician)"), [Joseph Brown](/wiki/Joseph_Brown_(astronomer) "Joseph Brown (astronomer)"), and [Moses Brown](/wiki/Moses_Brown "Moses Brown") were instrumental in moving the college to Providence, constructing its first building, and securing its endowment. Joseph became a professor of [natural philosophy](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy") at the college; John served as its treasurer from 1775 to 1796; and Nicholas Sr's son [Nicholas Brown Jr.](/wiki/Nicholas_Brown_Jr. "Nicholas Brown Jr.") succeeded his uncle as treasurer from 1796 to 1825.[[38]](#cite_note-43)

On September 8, 1803, the corporation voted, "That the donation of $5,000, if made to this College within one Year from the late Commencement, shall entitle the donor to name the College." The following year, the appeal was answered by College Treasurer Nicholas Brown Jr. In a letter dated September 6, 1804, Brown committed "a donation of Five Thousand Dollars to Rhode Island College, to remain in perpetuity as a fund for the establishment of a Professorship of Oratory and Belles Letters." In recognition of the gift, the corporation on the same day voted, "That this College be called and known in all future time by the Name of Brown University."[[39]](#cite_note-44) Over the years, the benefactions of Nicholas Brown Jr., totaled nearly $160,000 and included funds for building Hope College (1821–22) and Manning Hall (1834–35).

In 1904, the [John Carter Brown Library](/wiki/John_Carter_Brown_Library "John Carter Brown Library") was established as an independently funded research library on Brown's campus; the library's collection was founded on that of [John Carter Brown](/wiki/John_Carter_Brown "John Carter Brown"), son of Nicholas Brown Jr.

The Brown family was involved in various business ventures in Rhode Island, and accrued wealth both directly and indirectly from the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_Slave_Trade "Transatlantic Slave Trade"). The family was divided on the issue of slavery. John Brown had defended slavery, while Moses and Nicholas Brown Jr. were fervent [abolitionists](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States").

In 2003, under the tenure of President [Ruth Simmons](/wiki/Ruth_Simmons "Ruth Simmons"), the university established a steering committee to investigate these ties of the university to slavery and recommend a strategy to address them.[[40]](#cite_note-45)

#### American Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: American Revolution")]

With British vessels patrolling [Narragansett Bay](/wiki/Narragansett_Bay "Narragansett Bay") in the fall of 1776, the college library was moved out of Providence for safekeeping. During the subsequent [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), Brown's [University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall_(Brown_University) "University Hall (Brown University)") was used to house French and other revolutionary troops led by General [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and the [Comte de Rochambeau](/wiki/Comte_de_Rochambeau "Comte de Rochambeau") as they waited to commence the [march of 1781](/wiki/Washington-Rochambeau_Revolutionary_Route "Washington-Rochambeau Revolutionary Route") that led to the [Siege of Yorktown](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") and the [Battle of the Chesapeake](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Chesapeake "Battle of the Chesapeake"). This has been celebrated as marking the defeat of the British and the end of the war. The building functioned as barracks and hospital from December 10, 1776, to April 20, 1780, and as a hospital for French troops from June 26, 1780, to May 27, 1782.[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

A number of Brown's founders and alumni played roles in the American Revolution and subsequent founding of the United States. Brown's first chancellor, Stephen Hopkins, served as a delegate to the [Colonial Congress in Albany](/wiki/Albany_Congress "Albany Congress") in 1754, and to the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") from 1774 to 1776. James Manning represented Rhode Island at the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation"), while concurrently serving as Brown's first president.[[41]](#cite_note-46) Two of Brown's founders, William Ellery and Stephen Hopkins signed the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_independence "Declaration of independence").

[James Mitchell Varnum](/wiki/James_Mitchell_Varnum "James Mitchell Varnum"), who graduated from Brown with honors in 1769, served as one of General George Washington's [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") [brigadier generals](/wiki/Brigadier_general "Brigadier general") and later as [major general](/wiki/Major_general "Major general") in command of the entire [Rhode Island militia](/wiki/Rhode_Island_militia "Rhode Island militia"). Varnum is noted as the founder and commander of the [1st Rhode Island Regiment](/wiki/1st_Rhode_Island_Regiment "1st Rhode Island Regiment"), widely regarded as the first Black battalion in U.S. military history.[[42]](#cite_note-47) [David Howell](/wiki/David_Howell_(jurist) "David Howell (jurist)"), who graduated with an A.M. in 1769, served as a delegate to the [Continental Congress](/wiki/Continental_Congress "Continental Congress") from 1782 to 1785.

### Presidents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Presidents")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/JonathanMaxcy.jpg/250px-JonathanMaxcy.jpg)](/wiki/File:JonathanMaxcy.jpg)

Brown's 2nd President, [Jonathan Maxcy](/wiki/Jonathan_Maxcy "Jonathan Maxcy"), was the first alum to serve as president.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Francis_Wayland_by_George_Peter_Alexander_Healy_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Francis_Wayland_by_George_Peter_Alexander_Healy_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_Wayland_by_George_Peter_Alexander_Healy_(cropped).jpg)

Brown's 4th President, [Francis Wayland](/wiki/Francis_Wayland "Francis Wayland"), urged American universities to adopt a broader curriculum.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/DrRuthJSimmons.jpg/250px-DrRuthJSimmons.jpg)](/wiki/File:DrRuthJSimmons.jpg)

Brown's 18th President, [Ruth Simmons](/wiki/Ruth_Simmons "Ruth Simmons"), was the Ivy League's first [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") president.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Christina_paxson.jpg/250px-Christina_paxson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina_paxson.jpg)

Brown's 19th President, [Christina Paxson](/wiki/Christina_Paxson "Christina Paxson"), has served in the role since 2012.

Main article: [List of Presidents of Brown University](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_Brown_University "List of Presidents of Brown University")

Nineteen individuals have served as presidents of the university since its founding in 1764. Since 2012, [Christina Hull Paxson](/wiki/Christina_Hull_Paxson "Christina Hull Paxson") has served as president. Paxson had previously served as dean of [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")'s [School of Public and International Affairs](/wiki/Princeton_School_of_Public_and_International_Affairs "Princeton School of Public and International Affairs") and chair of Princeton's economics department.[[43]](#cite_note-48) Paxson's immediate predecessor, [Ruth Simmons](/wiki/Ruth_Simmons "Ruth Simmons"), is noted as the first [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") president of an Ivy League institution.[[44]](#cite_note-49) Other presidents of note include academic, [Vartan Gregorian](/wiki/Vartan_Gregorian "Vartan Gregorian"); and philosopher and economist, [Francis Wayland](/wiki/Francis_Wayland "Francis Wayland").

### New Curriculum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: New Curriculum")]

Main article: [Open Curriculum (Brown University)](/wiki/Open_Curriculum_(Brown_University) "Open Curriculum (Brown University)")

In 1966, the first Group Independent Study Project (GISP) at Brown was formed, involving 80 students and 15 professors. The GISP was inspired by student-initiated experimental schools, especially [San Francisco State College](/wiki/San_Francisco_State_University "San Francisco State University"), and sought ways to "put students at the center of their education" and "teach students how to think rather than just teaching facts".[[45]](#cite_note-:0-50)

Members of the GISP, [Ira Magaziner](/wiki/Ira_Magaziner "Ira Magaziner") and Elliot Maxwell published a paper of their findings titled, "Draft of a Working Paper for Education at Brown University."[[46]](#cite_note-51)[[45]](#cite_note-:0-50) The paper made proposals for a new curriculum, including [interdisciplinary](/wiki/Interdisciplinary "Interdisciplinary") freshman-year courses that would introduce "modes of thought", with instruction from faculty from different disciplines as well as for an end to letter grades. The following year Magaziner began organizing the student body to press for the reforms, organizing discussions and protests.[[47]](#cite_note-52)

In 1968, university president [Ray Heffner](/wiki/Ray_Heffner "Ray Heffner") established a Special Committee on Curricular Philosophy. Composed of administrators, the committee was tasked with developing specific reforms and producing recommendations. A report, produced by the committee, was presented to the faculty, which voted the New Curriculum into existence on May 7, 1969. Its key features included:[[48]](#cite_note-53)

* Modes of Thought courses for first-year students
* The introduction of interdisciplinary courses
* The abandonment of "general education" distribution requirements
* The Satisfactory/No Credit (S/NC) grading option
* The ABC/No Credit grading system, which eliminated pluses, minuses, and D's; a grade of "No Credit" (equivalent to F's at other institutions) would not appear on external transcripts.

The Modes of Thought course was discontinued early on, but the other elements remain in place. In 2006, the reintroduction of plus/minus grading was proposed in response to concerns regarding grade inflation. The idea was rejected by the College Curriculum Council after canvassing alumni, faculty, and students, including the original authors of the Magaziner-Maxwell Report.[[49]](#cite_note-54)

### "Slavery and Justice" report

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: \"Slavery and Justice\" report")]

In 2003, then-university president [Ruth Simmons](/wiki/Ruth_Simmons "Ruth Simmons") launched a steering committee to research Brown's eighteenth-century ties to slavery. In October 2006, the committee released a report documenting its findings.[[50]](#cite_note-55)[[51]](#cite_note-56)

Titled "Slavery and Justice", the document detailed the ways in which the university benefited both directly and indirectly from the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade") and the labor of enslaved people. The report also included seven recommendations for how the university should address this legacy.[[52]](#cite_note-:2-57) Brown has since completed a number of these recommendations including the establishment of its Center for the Study of Slavery and Justice, the construction of its *[Slavery Memorial](/wiki/Slavery_Memorial_(Brown_University) "Slavery Memorial (Brown University)")*, and the funding of a $10 million permanent endowment for [Providence Public Schools](/wiki/Providence_Public_School_District "Providence Public School District").[[52]](#cite_note-:2-57)[[53]](#cite_note-58)

The "Slavery and Justice" report marked the first major effort by an American university to address its ties to slavery and prompted other institutions to undertake similar processes.[[54]](#cite_note-59)[[55]](#cite_note-60)

### Interaction with the Trump administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Interaction with the Trump administration")]

On July 30, 2025, Brown struck a deal with the [Trump administration](/wiki/Second_presidency_of_Donald_Trump "Second presidency of Donald Trump") to restore federal research grant funding, after the administration froze it due to complaints mainly about antisemitism on the campus in the wake of [pro-Palestinian protests](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses"). This deal included $50 million to be paid over ten years to state workforce development programs, a vow to exclude transgender women from women's spaces, a commitment to combatting antisemitism, and renewed partnership with Israeli institutions, among other items.[[56]](#cite_note-trump_agreement-61)[[57]](#cite_note-62) This is the third settlement of its kind from a university to the administration, following Columbia University and the University of Pennsylvania.

Following the federal government's [offer of compliance with increased restrictions in exchange for preferential treatment in funding decisions](/wiki/Compact_for_Academic_Excellence_in_Higher_Education "Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education"), the university's leaders solicited comments from the university community. Once the period for comments concluded, they chose to reject the offer, becoming the second university to do so after [Massachusetts Institute of Technology](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology").[[58]](#cite_note-63)

### 2025 shooting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2025 shooting")]

Main article: [2025 Brown University shooting](/wiki/2025_Brown_University_shooting "2025 Brown University shooting")

On December 13, 2025, a [mass shooting](/wiki/Mass_shooting "Mass shooting") occurred in the Barus and Holley Engineering Building, killing two and injuring nine more.

## Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

Main article: [Coat of Arms of Brown University](/wiki/Coat_of_Arms_of_Brown_University "Coat of Arms of Brown University")

Brown's coat of arms was created in 1834. The prior year, president [Francis Wayland](/wiki/Francis_Wayland "Francis Wayland") had commissioned a committee to update the school's original seal to match the name the university had adopted in 1804. Central in the coat of arms is a white [escutcheon](/wiki/Escutcheon_(heraldry) "Escutcheon (heraldry)") divided into four sectors by a red cross. Within each sector of the coat of arms lies an open book. Above the shield is a crest consisting of the upper half of a [sun in splendor](/wiki/Sun_in_splendor "Sun in splendor") among the clouds atop a red and white [torse](/wiki/Torse "Torse").[[59]](#cite_note-64)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Brown%27s_University_Hall_in_2007.jpg/330px-Brown%27s_University_Hall_in_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown%27s_University_Hall_in_2007.jpg)

[University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall_(Brown_University) "University Hall (Brown University)"), Brown's oldest building, was constructed in 1770 and is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Das_%C3%B6stliche_Eingangstor_der_Brown_University.jpg/330px-Das_%C3%B6stliche_Eingangstor_der_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Das_%C3%B6stliche_Eingangstor_der_Brown_University.jpg)

Soldiers Memorial Gate (1921) long marked the eastern edge of Brown's campus.

Brown is the largest institutional landowner in Providence, with properties on College Hill and in the [Jewelry District](/wiki/Jewelry_District_(Providence) "Jewelry District (Providence)").[[60]](#cite_note-65) The university was built contemporaneously with the eighteenth and nineteenth-century precincts surrounding it, making Brown's campus tightly integrated into Providence's urban fabric. Among the noted architects who have shaped Brown's campus are [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White"), [Philip Johnson](/wiki/Philip_Johnson "Philip Johnson"), [Rafael Viñoly](/wiki/Rafael_Vi%C3%B1oly "Rafael Viñoly"), [Diller Scofidio + Renfro](/wiki/Diller_Scofidio_%2B_Renfro "Diller Scofidio + Renfro"), and [Robert A. M. Stern](/wiki/Robert_A._M._Stern "Robert A. M. Stern").[[61]](#cite_note-66)

### Main campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Main campus")]

Further information: [List of Brown University buildings](/wiki/List_of_Brown_University_buildings "List of Brown University buildings")

Brown's main campus, comprises 235 buildings and 143 acres (58 ha) in the [East Side](/wiki/East_Side,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "East Side, Providence, Rhode Island") neighborhood of College Hill. The university's central campus sits on a 15-acre (6.1 ha) block bounded by Waterman, Prospect, George, and [Thayer Streets](/wiki/Thayer_Street "Thayer Street"); newer buildings extend northward, eastward, and southward. Brown's core, historic campus, constructed primarily between 1770 and 1926, is defined by three greens: the Front or Quiet Green, the Main or College Green, and the Ruth J. Simmons Quadrangle (historically known as Lincoln Field).[[62]](#cite_note-campus_heritage2-67)[[63]](#cite_note-68) A brick and wrought-iron fence punctuated by decorative gates and arches traces the block's perimeter. This section of campus is primarily [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") and [Richardsonian Romanesque](/wiki/Richardsonian_Romanesque "Richardsonian Romanesque") in its architectural character.[[62]](#cite_note-campus_heritage2-67)

To the south of the central campus are academic buildings and residential quadrangles, including Wriston, Keeney, and Gregorian quadrangles. Immediately to the east of the campus core sit Sciences Park and Brown's [School of Engineering](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Engineering "Brown University School of Engineering"). North of the central campus are performing and visual arts facilities, life sciences labs, and the Pembroke Campus, which houses both dormitories and academic buildings. Facing the western edge of the central campus sit two of the Brown's seven libraries, the [John Hay Library](/wiki/John_Hay_Library "John Hay Library") and the [John D. Rockefeller Jr. Library](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr._Library "John D. Rockefeller Jr. Library").

The university's campus is contiguous with that of the [Rhode Island School of Design](/wiki/Rhode_Island_School_of_Design "Rhode Island School of Design"), which is located immediately to Brown's west, along the slope of College Hill.

#### Van Wickle Gates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Van Wickle Gates")]

Main article: [Van Wickle Gates](/wiki/Van_Wickle_Gates "Van Wickle Gates")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/The_iconic_Van_Wickle_Gates_at_Brown_University%2C_one_of_America%27s_prestigious_%22Ivy_League%22_colleges%2C_in_Providence%2C_the_capital_of%2C_and_largest_city_in%2C_Rhode_Island.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_iconic_Van_Wickle_Gates_at_Brown_University,_one_of_America%27s_prestigious_%22Ivy_League%22_colleges,_in_Providence,_the_capital_of,_and_largest_city_in,_Rhode_Island.jpg)

The [Van Wickle Gates](/wiki/Van_Wickle_Gates "Van Wickle Gates") stand at the crest of [College Hill](/wiki/College_Hill,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "College Hill, Providence, Rhode Island")

Built in 1901, the Van Wickle Gates are a set of wrought iron gates that stand at the western edge of Brown's campus. The larger main gate is flanked by two smaller side gates. At Convocation the central gate opens inward to admit the procession of new students; at Commencement, the gate opens outward for the procession of graduates.[[64]](#cite_note-69) A Brown superstition holds that students who walk through the central gate a second time prematurely will not graduate, although walking backward is said to cancel the hex.

### John Hay Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: John Hay Library")]

Main article: [John Hay Library](/wiki/John_Hay_Library "John Hay Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/John_Hay_Library_%28Brown%29.jpg/250px-John_Hay_Library_%28Brown%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Hay_Library_(Brown).jpg)

The [John Hay Library](/wiki/John_Hay_Library "John Hay Library") is home to rare books, special collections, and the university archives.

The John Hay Library is the second oldest library on campus.[[65]](#cite_note-70) Opened in 1910, the library is named for [John Hay](/wiki/John_Hay "John Hay") (class of 1858), private secretary to [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") and Secretary of State under [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") and [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"). The construction of the building was funded in large part by Hay's friend, [Andrew Carnegie](/wiki/Andrew_Carnegie "Andrew Carnegie"), who contributed half of the $300,000 cost of construction.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

The John Hay Library serves as the repository of the university's archives, rare books and manuscripts, and special collections. Noteworthy among the latter are the [Anne S. K. Brown Military Collection](/wiki/Anne_S._K._Brown_Military_Collection "Anne S. K. Brown Military Collection")[[67]](#cite_note-72) (described as "the foremost American collection of material devoted to the history and iconography of soldiers and soldiering"),[[68]](#cite_note-73) the Harris Collection of American Poetry and Plays (described as "the largest and most comprehensive collection of its kind in any research library"), the Lownes Collection of the History of Science (described as "one of the three most important private collections of books of science in America"), and the papers of [H. P. Lovecraft](/wiki/H._P._Lovecraft "H. P. Lovecraft"). The Hay Library is home to one of the broadest collections of [incunabula](/wiki/Incunable "Incunable") in the Americas, one of Brown's two [Shakespeare First Folios](/wiki/First_Folio "First Folio"), the manuscript of [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s *[Nineteen Eighty-Four](/wiki/Nineteen_Eighty-Four "Nineteen Eighty-Four"),* and three books [bound in human skin](/wiki/Anthropodermic_bibliopegy "Anthropodermic bibliopegy").[[69]](#cite_note-74)

### John Carter Brown Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: John Carter Brown Library")]

Main article: [John Carter Brown Library](/wiki/John_Carter_Brown_Library "John Carter Brown Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Entrance_to_the_John_Carter_Brown_Library_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Entrance_to_the_John_Carter_Brown_Library_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entrance_to_the_John_Carter_Brown_Library_(cropped).jpg)

The [John Carter Brown Library](/wiki/John_Carter_Brown_Library "John Carter Brown Library") is one of the world's leading repositories of books, maps, and manuscripts relating to the [colonial Americas](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas").[[70]](#cite_note-75)

Founded in 1846, the John Carter Brown Library is generally regarded as the world's leading collection of primary historical sources relating to the exploration and colonization of the Americas. While administered and funded separately from the university, the library has been owned by Brown and located on its campus since 1904.[[71]](#cite_note-76)

The library contains the best preserved of the eleven surviving copies of the [Bay Psalm Book](/wiki/Bay_Psalm_Book "Bay Psalm Book")—the earliest extant book printed in British North America and the most expensive printed book in the world.[[72]](#cite_note-77) Other holdings include a Shakespeare [First Folio](/wiki/First_Folio "First Folio") and the world's largest collection of 16th-century Mexican texts.[[73]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Manning_Hall%2C_Brown_University%2C_Providence%2C_Rhode_Island_-_20091108_straighten.jpg/250px-Manning_Hall%2C_Brown_University%2C_Providence%2C_Rhode_Island_-_20091108_straighten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manning_Hall,_Brown_University,_Providence,_Rhode_Island_-_20091108_straighten.jpg)

The galleries of Brown's anthropology museum, the [Haffenreffer](/wiki/Haffenreffer_Museum_of_Anthropology "Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology"), are located in Manning Hall.

### Haffenreffer Museum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Haffenreffer Museum")]

Main article: [Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/Haffenreffer_Museum_of_Anthropology "Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology")

The exhibition galleries of the Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology, Brown's teaching museum, are located in Manning Hall on the campus's main green. Its one million artifacts, available for research and educational purposes, are located at its Collections Research Center in [Bristol, Rhode Island](/wiki/Bristol,_Rhode_Island "Bristol, Rhode Island").[[74]](#cite_note-79) The museum's goal is to inspire creative and critical thinking about culture by fostering an interdisciplinary understanding of the material world. It provides opportunities for faculty and students to work with collections and the public, teaching through objects and programs in classrooms and exhibitions. The museum sponsors lectures and events in all areas of anthropology and also runs an extensive program of outreach to local schools.

### Annmary Brown Memorial

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Annmary Brown Memorial")]

Main article: [Annmary Brown Memorial](/wiki/Annmary_Brown_Memorial "Annmary Brown Memorial")

The Annmary Brown Memorial was constructed from 1903 to 1907 by the politician, Civil War veteran, and book collector General [Rush Hawkins](/wiki/Rush_Hawkins "Rush Hawkins"), as a mausoleum for his wife, Annmary Brown, a member of the Brown family. In addition to its crypt—the final repository for Brown and Hawkins—the Memorial includes works of art from Hawkins's private collection, including paintings by [Angelica Kauffman](/wiki/Angelica_Kauffman "Angelica Kauffman"), [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"), [Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart"), [Giovanni Battista Tiepolo](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo "Giovanni Battista Tiepolo"), [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West"), and [Eastman Johnson](/wiki/Eastman_Johnson "Eastman Johnson"), among others. His collection of over 450 [incunabula](/wiki/Incunable "Incunable") was relocated to the John Hay Library in 1990.[[75]](#cite_note-80) Today the Memorial is home to Brown's [Medieval Studies](/wiki/Medieval_studies "Medieval studies") and [Renaissance Studies](/wiki/Renaissance_studies "Renaissance studies") programs.

### The Walk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: The Walk")]

The Walk, a landscaped pedestrian corridor, connects the Pembroke Campus to the main campus. It runs parallel to [Thayer Street](/wiki/Thayer_Street "Thayer Street") and serves as a primary axis of campus, extending from Ruth Simmons Quadrangle at its southern terminus to the Meeting Street entrance to the Pembroke Campus at its northern end.[[76]](#cite_note-81)[[77]](#cite_note-82) The walk is bordered by departmental buildings as well as the [Lindemann Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Lindemann_Performing_Arts_Center "Lindemann Performing Arts Center") and [Granoff Center for the Creative Arts](/wiki/Perry_and_Marty_Granoff_Center_for_the_Creative_Arts "Perry and Marty Granoff Center for the Creative Arts")

The corridor is home to public art including sculptures by [Maya Lin](/wiki/Maya_Lin "Maya Lin") and [Tom Friedman](/wiki/Tom_Friedman_(artist) "Tom Friedman (artist)").[[78]](#cite_note-:1-83)

### Pembroke campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Pembroke campus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Brown_University_Pembroke_campus.jpg/250px-Brown_University_Pembroke_campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University_Pembroke_campus.jpg)

Three dormitories, Metcalf Hall (1919), Andrews Hall (1947), and Miller Hall (1910), formed the heart of Pembroke College and now serve as freshman residences.

The [Women's College in Brown University](/wiki/Pembroke_College_in_Brown_University "Pembroke College in Brown University"), known as Pembroke College, was founded in October 1891. Upon its 1971 merger with the College of Brown University, Pembroke's campus was absorbed into the larger Brown campus. The Pembroke campus is bordered by Meeting, Brown, Bowen, and Thayer Streets and sits three blocks north of Brown's central campus. The campus is dominated by brick architecture, largely of the [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") and [Victorian styles](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture"). The west side of the quadrangle comprises Pembroke Hall (1897), Smith-Buonanno Hall (1907), and Metcalf Hall (1919), while the east side comprises Alumnae Hall (1927) and Miller Hall (1910). The quadrangle culminates on the north with Andrews Hall (1947).

East Campus, centered on Hope and Charlesfield streets, originally served as the campus of [Bryant University](/wiki/Bryant_University "Bryant University"). In 1969, as Bryant was preparing to relocate to [Smithfield, Rhode Island](/wiki/Smithfield,_Rhode_Island "Smithfield, Rhode Island"), Brown purchased their Providence campus for $5 million. The transaction expanded the Brown campus by 10 acres (40,000 m2) and 26 buildings. In 1971, Brown renamed the area East Campus.[[79]](#cite_note-84) Today, the area is largely used for dormitories.

[Thayer Street](/wiki/Thayer_Street "Thayer Street") runs through Brown's main campus. As a commercial corridor frequented by students, Thayer is comparable to [Harvard Square](/wiki/Harvard_Square "Harvard Square") or Berkeley's [Telegraph Avenue](/wiki/Telegraph_Avenue "Telegraph Avenue"). [Wickenden Street](/wiki/Wickenden_Street "Wickenden Street"), in the adjacent [Fox Point](/wiki/Fox_Point,_Providence,_Rhode_Island "Fox Point, Providence, Rhode Island") neighborhood, is another commercial street similarly popular among students.

Built in 1925, [Brown Stadium](/wiki/Brown_Stadium "Brown Stadium")—the home of the school's football team—is located approximately a mile and a half northeast of the university's central campus.[[80]](#cite_note-85) Marston Boathouse, the home of Brown's crew teams, lies on the [Seekonk River](/wiki/Seekonk_River "Seekonk River"), to the southeast of campus. Brown's sailing teams are based out of the Ted Turner Sailing Pavilion at the [Edgewood Yacht Club](/wiki/Edgewood_Yacht_Club "Edgewood Yacht Club") in adjacent [Cranston](/wiki/Cranston,_Rhode_Island "Cranston, Rhode Island").

Since 2011, Brown's Warren Alpert Medical School has been located in Providence's historic [Jewelry District](/wiki/Jewelry_District_(Providence) "Jewelry District (Providence)"), near the medical campus of Brown's teaching hospitals, [Rhode Island Hospital](/wiki/Rhode_Island_Hospital "Rhode Island Hospital") and the [Women and Infants Hospital of Rhode Island](/wiki/Women_%26_Infants_Hospital_of_Rhode_Island "Women & Infants Hospital of Rhode Island"). Other university facilities, including [molecular medicine](/wiki/Molecular_medicine "Molecular medicine") labs and administrative offices, are likewise located in the area.[[81]](#cite_note-86)[[82]](#cite_note-87)

Brown's [School of Public Health](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Public_Health "Brown University School of Public Health") occupies a landmark modernist building along the [Providence River](/wiki/Providence_River "Providence River"). Other Brown properties include the 376-acre (1.52 km2) [Mount Hope Grant](/wiki/Mount_Hope_(Rhode_Island) "Mount Hope (Rhode Island)") in Bristol, Rhode Island, an important Native American site noted as a location of [King Philip's War](/wiki/King_Philip%27s_War "King Philip's War"). Brown's [Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/Haffenreffer_Museum_of_Anthropology "Haffenreffer Museum of Anthropology") Collection Research Center, particularly strong in Native American items, is located in the Mount Hope Grant.

### Sustainability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sustainability")]

Brown has committed to "minimize its energy use, reduce negative environmental impacts, and promote environmental stewardship."[[83]](#cite_note-Brown's_Energy_and_Environmental_Mission-88) Since 2010, the university has required all new buildings meet [LEED silver](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design") standards.[[84]](#cite_note-89) Between 2007 and 2018, Brown reduced its [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") by 27 percent; the majority of this reduction is attributable to the university's Thermal Efficiency Project which converted its central heating plant from a steam-powered system to a hot water-powered system.[[85]](#cite_note-90)

In 2020, Brown announced it had sold 90 percent of its [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") investments as part of a broader divestment from direct investments and managed funds that focus on [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels").[[86]](#cite_note-91) In 2021, the university adopted the goal of reducing quantifiable campus emissions by 75 percent by 2025 and achieving [carbon neutrality](/wiki/Carbon_neutrality "Carbon neutrality") by 2040.[[87]](#cite_note-92) Brown is a member of the Ivy Plus Sustainability Consortium, through which it has committed to best-practice sharing and the ongoing exchange of campus sustainability solutions along with other member institutions.[[88]](#cite_note-93)

According to the [A. W. Kuchler](/wiki/A._W._Kuchler "A. W. Kuchler") U.S. [potential natural vegetation](/wiki/Potential_natural_vegetation "Potential natural vegetation") types, Brown would have a dominant vegetation type of Appalachian [Oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") (*104*) with a dominant vegetation form of Eastern [Hardwood](/wiki/Hardwood "Hardwood") Forest (*25*).[[89]](#cite_note-Conservation_Biology_Institute-94)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Academics")]

### The College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: The College")]

Main article: [College of Brown University](/wiki/College_of_Brown_University "College of Brown University")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Brown_university_robinson_hall_2009a_crop_and_straighten.jpg/250px-Brown_university_robinson_hall_2009a_crop_and_straighten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_university_robinson_hall_2009a_crop_and_straighten.jpg)

Robinson Hall (1878) was designed by [Walker and Gould](/wiki/William_R._Walker_%26_Son "William R. Walker & Son") in the [Venetian Gothic](/wiki/Venetian_Gothic_architecture "Venetian Gothic architecture") style to house Brown's library.

Founded in 1764, the College is Brown's oldest school. About 7,200 undergraduate students are enrolled in the college [*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*], and 81 concentrations are offered. For the graduating class of 2020, the most popular concentrations were Computer Science, Economics, Biology, History, Applied Mathematics, International Relations, and Political Science. A quarter of Brown undergraduates complete more than one concentration before graduating.[[90]](#cite_note-95) If the existing programs do not align with their intended curricular interests, undergraduates may design and pursue independent concentrations.[[91]](#cite_note-96)

Around 35 percent [*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] of undergraduates pursue graduate or professional study immediately, 60 percent within 5 years, and 80 percent within 10 years.[[92]](#cite_note-admit-facts-97) For the Class of 2009, 56 percent of all undergraduate alumni have since earned graduate degrees. Among undergraduate alumni who go on to receive graduate degrees, the most common degrees earned are J.D. (16%), M.D. (14%), M.A. (14%), M.Sc. (14%), and Ph.D. (11%). The most common institutions from which undergraduate alumni earn graduate degrees are Brown University, [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), and [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").[[93]](#cite_note-:5-98)

The highest fields of employment for undergraduate alumni ten years after graduation are education and higher education (15%), medicine (9%), business and finance (9%), law (8%), and computing and technology (7%).[[93]](#cite_note-:5-98)

### Brown and RISD

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Brown and RISD")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/List_Art_Center%2C_Brown_University.jpg/250px-List_Art_Center%2C_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:List_Art_Center,_Brown_University.jpg)

The List Art Center, built 1969–71, designed by [Philip Johnson](/wiki/Philip_Johnson "Philip Johnson"), houses Brown's Department of Visual Art and the [David Winton Bell Gallery](/wiki/David_Winton_Bell_Gallery "David Winton Bell Gallery").

Since its 1893 relocation to College Hill, [Rhode Island School of Design](/wiki/Rhode_Island_School_of_Design "Rhode Island School of Design") (RISD) has bordered Brown to its west. Since 1900, Brown and RISD students have been able to cross-register at the two institutions, with Brown students permitted to take as many as four courses at RISD to count towards their Brown degree.[[94]](#cite_note-99) The two institutions partner to provide various student-life services and the two student bodies compose a synergy in the College Hill cultural scene.

#### Dual Degree Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Dual Degree Program")]

After several years of discussion between the two institutions and several students pursuing dual degrees unofficially, Brown and RISD formally established a five-year dual degree program in 2007, with the first class matriculating in the fall of 2008.[[95]](#cite_note-100) The Brown|RISD Dual Degree Program, among the most selective in the country, offered admission to 20 of the 725 applicants for the class entering in autumn 2020, for an acceptance rate of 2.7%.[[96]](#cite_note-101) The program combines the complementary strengths of the two institutions, integrating studio art and design at RISD with Brown's academic offerings. Students are admitted to the Dual Degree Program for a course lasting five years and culminating in both the Bachelor of Arts (A.B.) or Bachelor of Science (Sc.B.) degree from Brown and the Bachelor of Fine Arts (B.F.A.) degree from RISD. Prospective students must apply to the two schools separately and be accepted by separate admissions committees. Their application must then be approved by a third Brown|RISD joint committee.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Granoff_Center_%28Brown%29.jpg/250px-Granoff_Center_%28Brown%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Granoff_Center_(Brown).jpg)

The [Granoff Center](/wiki/Perry_and_Marty_Granoff_Center_for_the_Creative_Arts "Perry and Marty Granoff Center for the Creative Arts"), designed by [Diller Scofidio + Renfro](/wiki/Diller_Scofidio_%2B_Renfro "Diller Scofidio + Renfro"), hosts the annual Brown|RISD Dual Degree exhibition.

Admitted students spend the first year in residence at RISD completing its first-year Experimental and Foundation Studies curriculum while taking up to three Brown classes. Students spend their second year in residence at Brown, during which students take mainly Brown courses while starting on their RISD major requirements. In the third, fourth, and fifth years, students can elect to live at either school or off-campus, and course distribution is determined by the requirements of each student's unique combination of Brown concentration and RISD major. Program participants are noted for their creative and original approach to cross-disciplinary opportunities, combining, for example, industrial design with engineering, or anatomical illustration with human biology, or philosophy with sculpture, or architecture with urban studies. An annual "BRDD Exhibition" is a well-publicized and heavily attended event, drawing interest and attendees from the broader world of industry, design, the media, and the fine arts.

#### MADE Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: MADE Program")]

In 2020, the two schools announced the establishment of a new joint Master of Arts in Design Engineering program. Abbreviated as MADE, the program intends to combine RISD's programs in industrial design with Brown's programs in engineering. The program is administered through Brown's School of Engineering and RISD's Architecture and Design Division.[[97]](#cite_note-102)

### Theatre and playwriting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Theatre and playwriting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Marcus_Aurelius_statue_and_Lyman_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg/250px-Marcus_Aurelius_statue_and_Lyman_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcus_Aurelius_statue_and_Lyman_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg)

Lyman Hall, built 1890–92, houses the Department of Theatre Arts and Performance Studies.

Brown's theatre and playwriting programs are among the best-regarded in the country.[[98]](#cite_note-103)[[99]](#cite_note-104) Six Brown graduates have received the [Pulitzer Prize for Drama](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Drama "Pulitzer Prize for Drama"); [Alfred Uhry](/wiki/Alfred_Uhry "Alfred Uhry") '58, [Lynn Nottage](/wiki/Lynn_Nottage "Lynn Nottage") '86, [Ayad Akhtar](/wiki/Ayad_Akhtar "Ayad Akhtar") '93, [Nilo Cruz](/wiki/Nilo_Cruz "Nilo Cruz") '94, [Quiara Alegría Hudes](/wiki/Quiara_Alegr%C3%ADa_Hudes "Quiara Alegría Hudes") '04, and [Jackie Sibblies Drury](/wiki/Jackie_Sibblies_Drury "Jackie Sibblies Drury") MFA '04.[[100]](#cite_note-105) In *[American Theater](/wiki/Theatre_Communications_Group "Theatre Communications Group")* magazine's 2009 ranking of the most-produced American plays, Brown graduates occupied four of the top five places—Peter Nachtrieb '97, Rachel Sheinkin '89, [Sarah Ruhl](/wiki/Sarah_Ruhl "Sarah Ruhl") '97, and [Stephen Karam](/wiki/Stephen_Karam "Stephen Karam") '02.[[101]](#cite_note-106)[[102]](#cite_note-107)

The undergraduate concentration encompasses programs in theatre history, performance theory, playwriting, dramaturgy, acting, directing, dance, speech, and technical production. Applications for doctoral and master's degree programs are made through the University Graduate School. Master's degrees in acting and directing are pursued in conjunction with the Brown/Trinity Rep MFA program, which partners with the [Trinity Repertory Company](/wiki/Trinity_Repertory_Company "Trinity Repertory Company"), a local [regional theatre](/wiki/Regional_theater_in_the_United_States "Regional theater in the United States").[[103]](#cite_note-108) In January 2025, The Brown/Trinity Rep Master of Fine Arts Programs in Acting and Directing indefinitely paused its new student admissions, effectively ending the program.[[104]](#cite_note-109)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Fall_view_over_Brown_2.jpeg/250px-Fall_view_over_Brown_2.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Fall_view_over_Brown_2.jpeg)

Aerial view of the Brown University English department

### Writing programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Writing programs")]

Writing at Brown—fiction, non-fiction, poetry, playwriting, screenwriting, electronic writing, mixed media, and the undergraduate writing proficiency requirement—is catered for by various centers and degree programs, and a faculty that has long included nationally and internationally known authors. The undergraduate concentration in literary arts offers courses in fiction, poetry, screenwriting, literary hypermedia, and translation. Graduate programs include the fiction and poetry MFA writing programs in the literary arts department and the MFA playwriting program in the theatre arts and performance studies department. The non-fiction writing program is offered in the English department. Screenwriting and cinema narrativity courses are offered in the departments of literary arts and modern culture and media. The undergraduate writing proficiency requirement is supported by the Writing Center.

#### Author prizewinners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Author prizewinners")]

Alumni authors take their degrees across the spectrum of degree concentrations, but a gauge of the strength of writing at Brown is the number of major national writing prizes won. To note only winners since the year 2000: [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction")-winners [Jeffrey Eugenides](/wiki/Jeffrey_Eugenides "Jeffrey Eugenides") '82 (2003), [Marilynne Robinson](/wiki/Marilynne_Robinson "Marilynne Robinson") '66 (2005), and [Andrew Sean Greer](/wiki/Andrew_Sean_Greer "Andrew Sean Greer") '92 (2018); British [Orange Prize](/wiki/Orange_Prize "Orange Prize")-winners [Marilynne Robinson](/wiki/Marilynne_Robinson "Marilynne Robinson") '66 (2009) and [Madeline Miller](/wiki/Madeline_Miller "Madeline Miller") '00 (2012); [Pulitzer Prize for Drama](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Drama "Pulitzer Prize for Drama")-winners [Nilo Cruz](/wiki/Nilo_Cruz "Nilo Cruz") '94 (2003), [Lynn Nottage](/wiki/Lynn_Nottage "Lynn Nottage") '86 (twice, 2009, 2017), [Quiara Alegría Hudes](/wiki/Quiara_Alegr%C3%ADa_Hudes "Quiara Alegría Hudes") '04 (2012), [Ayad Akhtar](/wiki/Ayad_Akhtar "Ayad Akhtar") '93 (2013), and [Jackie Sibblies Drury](/wiki/Jackie_Sibblies_Drury "Jackie Sibblies Drury") MFA '04 (2019); [Pulitzer Prize for Biography](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Biography_or_Autobiography "Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography")-winners [David Kertzer](/wiki/David_Kertzer "David Kertzer") '69 (2015) and [Benjamin Moser](/wiki/Benjamin_Moser "Benjamin Moser") '98 (2020); [Pulitzer Prize for Journalism](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize")-winners [James Risen](/wiki/James_Risen "James Risen") '77 (2006), [Gareth Cook](/wiki/Gareth_Cook "Gareth Cook") '91 (2005), [Tony Horwitz](/wiki/Tony_Horwitz "Tony Horwitz") '80 (1995), [Usha Lee McFarling](/wiki/Usha_Lee_McFarling "Usha Lee McFarling") '89 (2007), [David Rohde](/wiki/David_Rohde "David Rohde") '90 (1996), [Kathryn Schulz](/wiki/Kathryn_Schulz "Kathryn Schulz") '96 (2016), and [Alissa J. Rubin](/wiki/Alissa_J._Rubin "Alissa J. Rubin") '80 (2016); [Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_General_Nonfiction "Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction")-winner [James Forman Jr.](/wiki/James_Forman_Jr. "James Forman Jr.") '88 (2018); [Pulitzer Prize for History](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_General_Nonfiction "Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction")-winner [Marcia Chatelain](/wiki/Marcia_Chatelain "Marcia Chatelain") PhD '08 (2021); [Pulitzer Prize for Criticism](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_General_Nonfiction "Pulitzer Prize for General Nonfiction")-winner [Salamishah Tillet](/wiki/Salamishah_Tillet "Salamishah Tillet") MAT '97 (2022); and [Pulitzer Prize for Poetry](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Poetry "Pulitzer Prize for Poetry")-winner [Peter Balakian](/wiki/Peter_Balakian "Peter Balakian") PhD '80 (2016)[[105]](#cite_note-110)[[106]](#cite_note-111)

### Computer science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Computer science")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Computing_Laboratory%2C_Brown_University_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Computing_Laboratory%2C_Brown_University_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Computing_Laboratory,_Brown_University_(cropped).jpg)

The Brown Computing Laboratory, designed by [Philip Johnson](/wiki/Philip_Johnson "Philip Johnson")

Brown began offering computer science courses through the departments of Economics and Applied Mathematics in 1956 when it acquired an IBM machine. Brown added an [IBM 650](/wiki/IBM_650 "IBM 650") in January 1958, the only one of its type between Hartford and Boston. In 1960, Brown opened its first dedicated computer building, the [Brown University Computing Laboratory](/wiki/Brown_University_Computing_Laboratory "Brown University Computing Laboratory"). The facility, designed by [Philip Johnson](/wiki/Philip_Johnson "Philip Johnson"), received an [IBM 7070](/wiki/IBM_7070 "IBM 7070") computer the following year. The first undergraduate Computer Science degrees were awarded in 1974. Brown granted computer sciences full Departmental status in 1979. In 2009, IBM and Brown announced the installation of a supercomputer (by teraflops standards), the most powerful in the southeastern New England region.[[107]](#cite_note-112)

In the 1960s, [Andries van Dam](/wiki/Andries_van_Dam "Andries van Dam"), along with [Ted Nelson](/wiki/Ted_Nelson "Ted Nelson") and [Bob Wallace](/wiki/Bob_Wallace_(computer_scientist) "Bob Wallace (computer scientist)") invented The [Hypertext Editing Systems](/wiki/Hypertext_Editing_System "Hypertext Editing System"), [HES](/wiki/Hypertext_Editing_System "Hypertext Editing System") and [FRESS](/wiki/FRESS "FRESS") while at Brown. Nelson coined the word *[hypertext](/wiki/Hypertext "Hypertext")* while Van Dam's students helped originate [XML](/wiki/XML "XML"), [XSLT](/wiki/XSLT "XSLT"), and related Web standards. Among the school's computer science alumni are principal architect of the [Classic Mac OS](/wiki/Classic_Mac_OS "Classic Mac OS"), [Andy Hertzfeld](/wiki/Andy_Hertzfeld "Andy Hertzfeld"); principal architect of the [Intel 80386](/wiki/Intel_80386 "Intel 80386") and [Intel 80486](/wiki/Intel_80486 "Intel 80486") microprocessors, [John Crawford](/wiki/John_Crawford_(engineer) "John Crawford (engineer)"); former CEO of [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [John Sculley](/wiki/John_Sculley "John Sculley"); and digital effects programmer [Masi Oka](/wiki/Masi_Oka "Masi Oka").[[108]](#cite_note-113)[[109]](#cite_note-114) Other alumni include former CS department head at MIT, [John Guttag](/wiki/John_Guttag "John Guttag"); software-defined networking pioneer [Scott Shenker](/wiki/Scott_Shenker "Scott Shenker"); [Workday](/wiki/Workday,_Inc. "Workday, Inc.") founder, [Aneel Bhusri](/wiki/Aneel_Bhusri "Aneel Bhusri"); [MongoDB](/wiki/MongoDB_Inc. "MongoDB Inc.") founder [Eliot Horowitz](/wiki/Eliot_Horowitz "Eliot Horowitz"); [Figma](/wiki/Figma_(software) "Figma (software)") founders [Dylan Field](/wiki/Dylan_Field "Dylan Field") and Evan Wallace (the latter of whom also created [esbuild](/wiki/Esbuild "Esbuild")); [OpenSea](/wiki/OpenSea "OpenSea") founder [Devin Finzer](/wiki/Devin_Finzer "Devin Finzer"); and [Edward D. Lazowska](/wiki/Edward_D._Lazowska "Edward D. Lazowska"), professor and Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation Chair emeritus at the [University of Washington](/wiki/University_of_Washington "University of Washington").[[110]](#cite_note-115)

Between 2012 and 2018, the number of concentrators in CS tripled.[[111]](#cite_note-116) In 2017, computer science overtook economics as the school's most popular undergraduate concentration.[[112]](#cite_note-117)

### Applied mathematics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Applied mathematics")]

Brown's program in [applied mathematics](/wiki/Applied_mathematics "Applied mathematics") was established in 1941 making it the oldest such program in the United States.[[10]](#cite_note-:9-11)[[113]](#cite_note-118) The division is highly ranked and regarded nationally.[[114]](#cite_note-119) Among the 67 recipients of the [Timoshenko Medal](/wiki/Timoshenko_Medal "Timoshenko Medal"), 22 have been affiliated with Brown's applied mathematics division as faculty, researchers, or students.[[f]](#cite_note-120)

### The Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: The Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Rhode_Island_Hall.jpg/250px-Rhode_Island_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rhode_Island_Hall.jpg)

[Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture") Rhode Island Hall (1840) on the Main Green is home to the [Joukowsky Institute](/wiki/Joukowsky_Institute_for_Archaeology_and_the_Ancient_World "Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Brown_University_Wilbour_Hall.jpg/250px-Brown_University_Wilbour_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University_Wilbour_Hall.jpg)

The Department of Egyptology and Assyriology in Wilbour Hall (1888). Wilbour Hall is named for Egyptologist [Charles Edwin Wilbour](/wiki/Charles_Edwin_Wilbour "Charles Edwin Wilbour") (class of 1854).

Main article: [Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World](/wiki/Joukowsky_Institute_for_Archaeology_and_the_Ancient_World "Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World")

Established in 2004, the Joukowsky Institute for Archaeology and the Ancient World is Brown's interdisciplinary research center for archeology and ancient studies. The institute pursues fieldwork, excavations, regional surveys, and academic study of the archaeology and art of the ancient Mediterranean, Egypt, and Western Asia from the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") to the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus").[[115]](#cite_note-121) The institute has a very active fieldwork profile, with faculty-led excavations and regional surveys presently in [Petra](/wiki/Petra "Petra") (Jordan), [Abydos](/wiki/Abydos,_Egypt "Abydos, Egypt") (Egypt), Turkey, Sudan, Italy, Mexico, Guatemala, [Montserrat](/wiki/Montserrat "Montserrat"), and Providence.

The Joukowsky Institute's faculty includes cross-appointments from the departments of Egyptology, Assyriology, Classics, Anthropology, and History of Art and Architecture. Faculty research and publication areas include Greek and Roman art and architecture, landscape archaeology, urban and religious architecture of the Levant, Roman provincial studies, the Aegean Bronze Age, and the archaeology of the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"). The institute offers visiting teaching appointments and postdoctoral fellowships which have, in recent years, included Near Eastern Archaeology and Art, [Classical Archaeology](/wiki/Classical_archaeology "Classical archaeology") and Art, Islamic Archaeology and Art, and Archaeology and Media Studies.

**Egyptology and Assyriology**

Facing the Joukowsky Institute, across the Front Green, is the Department of Egyptology and Assyriology, formed in 2006 by the merger of Brown's departments of Egyptology and History of Mathematics. It is one of only a handful of such departments in the United States.[[116]](#cite_note-122) The curricular focus is on three principal areas: [Egyptology](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology"), [Assyriology](/wiki/Assyriology "Assyriology"), and the history of the ancient exact sciences (astronomy, astrology, and mathematics). Many courses in the department are open to all Brown undergraduates without prerequisites and include archaeology, languages, history, and Egyptian and [Mesopotamian religions](/wiki/Ancient_Mesopotamian_religion "Ancient Mesopotamian religion"), literature, and science. Students concentrating in the department choose a track of either Egyptology or Assyriology. Graduate-level study comprises three tracks to the doctoral degree: Egyptology, Assyriology, or the History of the Exact Sciences in Antiquity.

### The Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: The Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs")]

Main article: [Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs](/wiki/Watson_Institute_for_International_and_Public_Affairs "Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Watson_Institute_%28Brown%29_6.jpg/250px-Watson_Institute_%28Brown%29_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Watson_Institute_(Brown)_6.jpg)

The main building at the [Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs](/wiki/Watson_Institute_for_International_and_Public_Affairs "Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs") was designed by [Rafael Viñoly](/wiki/Rafael_Vi%C3%B1oly "Rafael Viñoly") in 2001

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Stephen_Robert_Hall%2C_Brown_University_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Stephen_Robert_Hall%2C_Brown_University_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Robert_Hall,_Brown_University_(cropped).jpg)

Stephen Robert Hall (2018) at the Watson Institute, was designed by [Toshiko Mori](/wiki/Toshiko_Mori "Toshiko Mori")

The Watson Institute for International and Public Affairs, Brown's center for the study of global Issues and public affairs, is one of the leading institutes of its type in the country. The institute occupies facilities designed by Uruguayan architect [Rafael Viñoly](/wiki/Rafael_Vi%C3%B1oly "Rafael Viñoly") and Japanese architect [Toshiko Mori](/wiki/Toshiko_Mori "Toshiko Mori"). The institute was initially endowed by [Thomas Watson Jr.](/wiki/Thomas_J._Watson_Jr. "Thomas J. Watson Jr.") (Class of 1937), former [Ambassador to the Soviet Union](/wiki/Ambassador_to_the_Soviet_Union "Ambassador to the Soviet Union") and longtime president of [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM").

Institute faculty and faculty emeritus include [Italian prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") and [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") president [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi"),[[117]](#cite_note-123) Brazilian president [Fernando Henrique Cardoso](/wiki/Fernando_Henrique_Cardoso "Fernando Henrique Cardoso"),[[118]](#cite_note-124) Chilean president [Ricardo Lagos Escobar](/wiki/Ricardo_Lagos "Ricardo Lagos"),[[119]](#cite_note-125) Mexican novelist and statesman [Carlos Fuentes](/wiki/Carlos_Fuentes "Carlos Fuentes"),[[120]](#cite_note-126) Brazilian statesman and United Nations commission head [Paulo Sérgio Pinheiro](/wiki/Paulo_S%C3%A9rgio_Pinheiro "Paulo Sérgio Pinheiro"),[[121]](#cite_note-127) Indian foreign minister and ambassador to the United States [Nirupama Rao](/wiki/Nirupama_Rao "Nirupama Rao"),[[122]](#cite_note-128) American diplomat and [Dayton Peace Accords](/wiki/Dayton_Agreement "Dayton Agreement") author [Richard Holbrooke](/wiki/Richard_Holbrooke "Richard Holbrooke") (Class of 1962),[[123]](#cite_note-129) and [Sergei Khrushchev](/wiki/Sergei_Khrushchev "Sergei Khrushchev"),[[124]](#cite_note-130) editor of the papers of his father [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"), leader of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").

The institute's curricular interest is organized into the principal themes of development, security, and governance—with further focuses on globalization, economic uncertainty, security threats, [environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_degradation "Environmental degradation"), and poverty.
