Six Brown undergraduate concentrations are hosted by the Watson Institute: [Development Studies](/wiki/Development_studies "Development studies"), International and Public Affairs, International Relations, Latin American and Caribbean Studies, Middle East Studies, Public Policy, and South Asian Studies. Graduate programs offered at the Watson Institute include the Graduate Program in Development (Ph.D.) and the Master of Public Affairs (M.P.A) Program. The institute also offers postdoctoral, professional development, and global outreach programming. In support of these programs, the institute houses various centers, including the Brazil Initiative, Brown-India Initiative, China Initiative, Middle East Studies Center, The Center for Latin American and Caribbean Studies (CLACS), and the Taubman Center for Public Policy. In recent years, the most internationally cited product of the Watson Institute has been its [Costs of War Project](/wiki/Casualties_of_the_Iraq_War "Casualties of the Iraq War"), first released in 2011 and continuously updated since. The project comprises a team of economists, anthropologists, political scientists, legal experts, and physicians, and seeks to calculate the economic costs, human casualties, and impact on civil liberties of the wars in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%932021) "War in Afghanistan (2001–2021)"), and [Pakistan](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Insurgency in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa") since 2001.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

### The School of Engineering

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: The School of Engineering")]

Main article: [Brown University School of Engineering](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Engineering "Brown University School of Engineering")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Brown_University_Engineering_Research_Center.jpg/250px-Brown_University_Engineering_Research_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University_Engineering_Research_Center.jpg)

The Brown University Engineering Research Center, completed in 2018 and designed by [KieranTimberlake](/wiki/KieranTimberlake "KieranTimberlake")[[126]](#cite_note-132)

Established in 1847, Brown's engineering program is the oldest in the Ivy League and the third oldest civilian engineering program in the country.[[g]](#cite_note-133) In 1916, Brown's departments of electrical, mechanical, and civil engineering were merged into a single Division of Engineering. In 2010 the division was elevated to a School of Engineering.[[127]](#cite_note-134)

Engineering at Brown is especially interdisciplinary. The school is organized without the traditional departments or boundaries found at most schools and follows a model of connectivity between disciplines—including biology, medicine, physics, chemistry, computer science, the humanities, and the social sciences. The school practices an innovative clustering of faculties in which engineers team with non-engineers to bring a convergence of ideas.

Student teams have launched two [CubeSats](/wiki/CubeSat "CubeSat") with the support of the School of Engineering. Brown Space Engineering developed [EQUiSat](/wiki/EQUiSat "EQUiSat") a 1U satellite, and another interdisciplinary team developed [SBUDNIC](/wiki/SBUDNIC "SBUDNIC") a 3U satellite.[[128]](#cite_note-135)[[129]](#cite_note-136)

### IE Brown Executive MBA Dual Degree Program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: IE Brown Executive MBA Dual Degree Program")]

Since 2009, Brown has developed an Executive MBA program in conjunction with one of the leading Business Schools in Europe, [IE Business School](/wiki/IE_Business_School "IE Business School") in Madrid. This relationship has since strengthened resulting in both institutions offering a dual degree program.[[130]](#cite_note-137) In this partnership, Brown provides its traditional coursework while IE provides most of the business-related subjects making a differentiated alternative program to other Ivy League's EMBAs.[[131]](#cite_note-138) The cohort typically consists of 25–30 EMBA candidates from some 20 countries.[[132]](#cite_note-139) Classes are held in Providence, [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") and Online.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Pembroke_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg/250px-Pembroke_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pembroke_Hall_at_Brown_University.jpg)

Pembroke Hall (1897) houses the administrative offices of the [Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women](/wiki/Pembroke_Center_for_Teaching_and_Research_on_Women "Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women").

### The Pembroke Center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: The Pembroke Center")]

Main article: [Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women](/wiki/Pembroke_Center_for_Teaching_and_Research_on_Women "Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women")

The Pembroke Center for Teaching and Research on Women was established at Brown in 1981 by [Joan Wallach Scott](/wiki/Joan_Wallach_Scott "Joan Wallach Scott") as an interdisciplinary research center on gender.[[133]](#cite_note-140) The center is named for Pembroke College, Brown's former women's college, and is affiliated with Brown's [Sarah Doyle Women's Center](/wiki/Sarah_Doyle_Women%27s_Center "Sarah Doyle Women's Center"). The Pembroke Center supports Brown's undergraduate concentration in [Gender and Sexuality Studies](/wiki/Gender_studies "Gender studies"), post-doctoral research fellowships, the annual Pembroke Seminar, and other academic programs. It also manages various collections, archives, and resources, including the Elizabeth Weed Feminist Theory Papers and the Christine Dunlap Farnham Archive.

### The Graduate School

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: The Graduate School")]

Main article: [Brown University Graduate School](/wiki/Brown_University_Graduate_School "Brown University Graduate School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Brown%27s_Open_Curriculum_50_years_picnic.jpg/250px-Brown%27s_Open_Curriculum_50_years_picnic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown%27s_Open_Curriculum_50_years_picnic.jpg)

[Sayles Hall](/wiki/Sayles_Memorial_Hall "Sayles Memorial Hall") on the Main Green

Brown introduced graduate courses in the 1870s and granted its first advanced degrees in 1888. The university established a Graduate Department in 1903 and a full Graduate School in 1927.[[134]](#cite_note-141)

With an enrollment of approximately 2,600 students, the school currently offers 33 and 51 master's and doctoral programs, respectively. The school additionally offers a number of fifth-year master's programs.[[135]](#cite_note-142) Overall, admission to the Graduate School is most competitive with an acceptance rate averaging at approximately 9 percent in recent years.

### Carney Institute for Brain Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Carney Institute for Brain Science")]

Main article: [Carney Institute for Brain Science](/wiki/Carney_Institute_for_Brain_Science "Carney Institute for Brain Science")

The Robert J. & Nancy D. Carney Institute for Brain Science is Brown's cross-departmental neuroscience [research institute](/wiki/Research_institute "Research institute"). The institute's core focus areas include [brain-computer interfaces](/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface "Brain–computer interface") and [computational neuroscience](/wiki/Computational_neuroscience "Computational neuroscience"); additional areas of focus include research into mechanisms of [cell death](/wiki/Cell_death "Cell death") with the interest of developing therapies for [neurodegenerative diseases](/wiki/Neurodegeneration "Neurodegeneration").

The Carney Institute was founded by [John Donoghue](/wiki/John_Donoghue_(neuroscientist) "John Donoghue (neuroscientist)") in 2009 as the Brown Institute for Brain Science and renamed in 2018 in recognition of a $100 million gift.[[136]](#cite_note-providencejournal_2018-04-182-143) The donation, one of the largest in the university's history, established the institute as one of the best-endowed university neuroscience programs in the country.[[137]](#cite_note-:04-144)

### Alpert Medical School

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Alpert Medical School")]

Main article: [Alpert Medical School](/wiki/Alpert_Medical_School "Alpert Medical School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Alpert_Medical_School%2C_Providence%2C_Rhode_Island.jpg/250px-Alpert_Medical_School%2C_Providence%2C_Rhode_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alpert_Medical_School,_Providence,_Rhode_Island.jpg)

The [Alpert Medical School](/wiki/Alpert_Medical_School "Alpert Medical School") building on Richmond Street

Established in 1811, Brown's Alpert Medical School is the fourth oldest medical school in the Ivy League.[[12]](#cite_note-:13-13)[[h]](#cite_note-145)

In 1827, medical instruction was suspended by President [Francis Wayland](/wiki/Francis_Wayland "Francis Wayland") after the program's faculty declined to follow a new policy requiring students to live on campus. The program was reorganized in 1972; the first M.D. degrees from the new Program in Medicine were awarded to a graduating class of 58 students in 1975. In 1991, the school was officially renamed the Brown University School of Medicine, then renamed once more to Brown Medical School in October 2000.[[138]](#cite_note-146) In January 2007, entrepreneur and philanthropist [Warren Alpert](/wiki/Warren_Alpert "Warren Alpert") donated $100 million to the school. In recognition of the gift, the school's name was changed to the Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University.

In 2020, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranked Brown's medical school the 9th most selective in the country, with an acceptance rate of 2.8%.[[139]](#cite_note-usnews.com-147) *U.S. News* ranks the school 38th for research and 35th for primary care.[[140]](#cite_note-148)

Brown's medical school is known especially for its eight-year [Program in Liberal Medical Education](/wiki/Program_in_Liberal_Medical_Education "Program in Liberal Medical Education") (PLME), an eight-year combined baccalaureate-M.D. medical program. Inaugurated in 1984, the program is one of the most selective and renowned programs of its type in the country, offering admission to only 2% of applicants in 2021.[[141]](#cite_note-:6-149)

Since 1976, the Early Identification Program (EIP) has encouraged Rhode Island residents to pursue careers in medicine by recruiting sophomores from [Providence College](/wiki/Providence_College "Providence College"), [Rhode Island College](/wiki/Rhode_Island_College "Rhode Island College"), the [University of Rhode Island](/wiki/University_of_Rhode_Island "University of Rhode Island"), and [Tougaloo College](/wiki/Tougaloo_College "Tougaloo College"). In 2004, the school once again began to accept applications from premedical students at other colleges and universities via [AMCAS](/wiki/AMCAS "AMCAS") like most other medical schools. The medical school also offers M.D./PhD, M.D./[M.P.H.](/wiki/Master_of_Public_Health "Master of Public Health") and M.D./[M.P.P.](/wiki/Master_of_Public_Policy "Master of Public Policy") dual degree programs.

### School of Public Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: School of Public Health")]

Main article: [Brown University School of Public Health](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Public_Health "Brown University School of Public Health")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/121_South_Main_St_and_Providence_River.jpg/250px-121_South_Main_St_and_Providence_River.jpg)](/wiki/File:121_South_Main_St_and_Providence_River.jpg)

The primary building of the [Brown University School of Public Health](/wiki/Brown_University_School_of_Public_Health "Brown University School of Public Health") viewed from across the [Providence River](/wiki/Providence_River "Providence River")

Brown's School of Public Health grew out of the Alpert Medical School's Department of Community Health and was officially founded in 2013 as an independent school.[[142]](#cite_note-150)[[143]](#cite_note-:02-151) The school issues undergraduate (A.B., Sc.B.), graduate (M.P.H., Sc.M., A.M.), doctoral (Ph.D.), and dual-degrees (M.P.H./M.P.A., M.D./M.P.H.).[[144]](#cite_note-152)

### Online programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Online programs")]

The Brown University School of Professional Studies currently offers [blended learning](/wiki/Blended_learning "Blended learning") Executive master's degrees in [Healthcare Leadership](/wiki/Health_administration "Health administration"), Cyber Security, and Science and Technology Leadership.[[145]](#cite_note-153) The master's degrees are designed to help students who have a job and life outside of academia to progress in their respective fields. The students meet in Providence every 6–7 weeks for a weekly seminar each trimester.

The university has also invested in [MOOC](/wiki/MOOC "MOOC") development starting in 2013, when two courses, *Archeology's Dirty Little Secrets* and *The Fiction of Relationship*, both of which received thousands of students.[[146]](#cite_note-154) However, after a year of courses, the university broke its contract with [Coursera](/wiki/Coursera "Coursera") and revamped its online persona and MOOC development department. By 2017, the university released new courses on [edx](/wiki/EdX "EdX"), two of which were *The Ethics of Memory* and *Artful Medicine: Art's Power to Enrich Patient Care*. In January 2018, Brown published its first "game-ified" course called *Fantastic Places, Unhuman Humans: Exploring Humanity Through Literature*, which featured out-of-platform games to help learners understand materials, as well as a story-line that immerses users into a fictional world to help characters along their journey.[[147]](#cite_note-155)

### Undergraduate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Undergraduate")]

Undergraduate admission to Brown University is considered "most selective" by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report").*[[150]](#cite_note-158) For the undergraduate class of 2026, Brown received 50,649 applications—the largest applicant pool in the university's history and a 9% increase from the prior year. Of these applicants, 2,560 were admitted for an acceptance rate of 5.0%, the lowest in the university's history.[[151]](#cite_note-:10-159)

In 2021, the university reported a yield rate of 69%.[[152]](#cite_note-160) For the academic year 2019–20 the university received 2,030 transfer applications, of which 5.8% were accepted.[[153]](#cite_note-:3-161)

Brown's admissions policy is currently stipulated [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for all domestic first-year applicants,[[154]](#cite_note-162) but will be extended to international first-year applicants starting with the Class of 2029.[[155]](#cite_note-163) In 2017, Brown announced that loans would be eliminated from all undergraduate financial aid awards starting in 2018–2019, as part of a new $30 million campaign called the *Brown Promise*.[[156]](#cite_note-164) In 2016–17, the university awarded need-based scholarships worth $120.5 million. The average need-based award for the class of 2020 was $47,940.[[157]](#cite_note-165)

### Graduate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Graduate")]

In 2017, the Graduate School accepted 11% of 9,215 applicants.[[158]](#cite_note-166) In 2021, Brown received a record 948 applications for roughly 90 spots in its Master of Public Health Degree.[[159]](#cite_note-167)

In 2020, *U.S. News* ranked Brown's [Warren Alpert Medical School](/wiki/Alpert_Medical_School "Alpert Medical School") the 9th most selective in the country, with an acceptance rate of 2.8 percent.[[160]](#cite_note-168)

Brown University is [accredited](/wiki/Higher_education_accreditation_in_the_United_States "Higher education accreditation in the United States") by the [New England Commission of Higher Education](/wiki/New_England_Commission_of_Higher_Education "New England Commission of Higher Education").[[170]](#cite_note-178) For their 2021 rankings, The Wall Street Journal/Times Higher Education ranked Brown 5th in the "Best Colleges 2021" edition.[[171]](#cite_note-:12-179)

The *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* magazine annual ranking of "America's Top Colleges 2022"—which ranked 600 research universities, liberal arts colleges and service academies—ranked Brown 19th overall and 18th among universities.[[172]](#cite_note-180)

*[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranked Brown 9th among national universities in its 2023 edition.[[173]](#cite_note-:11-181) The 2022 edition also ranked Brown 2nd for undergraduate teaching, 25th in Most Innovative Schools, and 14th in Best Value Schools.[[174]](#cite_note-182)

*[Washington Monthly](/wiki/Washington_Monthly "Washington Monthly")* ranked Brown 30th in 2024 among 438 national universities in the U.S. based on its contribution to the public good, as measured by social mobility, research, and promoting public service.[[175]](#cite_note-183)

In 2022, *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* ranks Brown 129th globally.

In 2014, *Forbes* magazine ranked Brown 7th on its list of "America's Most Entrepreneurial Universities."[[176]](#cite_note-184) The *Forbes* analysis looked at the ratio of "alumni and students who have identified themselves as founders and business owners on LinkedIn" and the total number of alumni and students. LinkedIn particularized the *Forbes* rankings, placing Brown third (between [MIT](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") and [Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University")) among "Best Undergraduate Universities for Software Developers at Startups." LinkedIn's methodology involved a career-path examination of "millions of alumni profiles" in its membership database.[[177]](#cite_note-185)

In 2016, 2017, 2018, and 2021 the university produced the most [Fulbright](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program") recipients of any university in the nation.[[178]](#cite_note-186)[[179]](#cite_note-187)[[180]](#cite_note-188) Brown has also produced the 7th most Rhodes Scholars of all colleges and universities in the United States.[[181]](#cite_note-189)

In 2025 the university ranked 229 of 257 top colleges in free speech rankings by the [Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression](/wiki/Foundation_for_Individual_Rights_and_Expression "Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression") and "College Pulse", after ranking at 69 in 2024 and at 114 in 2022/2023.[[182]](#cite_note-190)[[183]](#cite_note-191)[[184]](#cite_note-192)

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Research")]

Brown is a member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") since 1933 and is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very High Research Activity".[[185]](#cite_note-193)[[186]](#cite_note-194) In FY 2017, Brown spent $212.3 million on research and was ranked 103rd in the United States by total R&D expenditure by [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation").[[187]](#cite_note-195)[[188]](#cite_note-196) In 2021 Brown's School of Public Health received the 4th most funding in [NIH awards](/wiki/NIH_grant "NIH grant") among schools of public health in the U.S.[[189]](#cite_note-197)

### Spring weekend

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Spring weekend")]

Main article: [Spring Weekend](/wiki/Spring_Weekend "Spring Weekend")

Established in 1950, Spring Weekend is an annual spring music festival for students. Historical performers at the festival have included [Ella Fitzgerald](/wiki/Ella_Fitzgerald "Ella Fitzgerald"), [Dizzy Gillespie](/wiki/Dizzy_Gillespie "Dizzy Gillespie"), [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles"), [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Janis Joplin](/wiki/Janis_Joplin "Janis Joplin"), [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), and U2. More recent headliners include [Kendrick Lamar](/wiki/Kendrick_Lamar "Kendrick Lamar"), [Young Thug](/wiki/Young_Thug "Young Thug"), [Daniel Caesar](/wiki/Daniel_Caesar "Daniel Caesar"), [Anderson .Paak](/wiki/Anderson_.Paak "Anderson .Paak"), [Mitski](/wiki/Mitski "Mitski"), [Aminé](/wiki/Amin%C3%A9 "Aminé"), and [Mac DeMarco](/wiki/Mac_DeMarco "Mac DeMarco").[[191]](#cite_note-201)[[192]](#cite_note-202)[[193]](#cite_note-203)[[194]](#cite_note-204) Since 1960, Spring Weekend has been organized by the student-run Brown Concert Agency.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Main_Green_at_Brown_University.jpg/250px-Main_Green_at_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_Green_at_Brown_University.jpg)

Many Spring Weekend events are hosted on Brown's Main Green.[[195]](#cite_note-205)

### Residential and Greek societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Residential and Greek societies")]

Approximately 12 percent of Brown students participate in [Greek Life](/wiki/Fraternities_and_sororities "Fraternities and sororities").[[196]](#cite_note-206) The university recognizes thirteen active Greek organizations. Since the early 1950s, all Greek organizations on campus have been located in Wriston Quadrangle.[[197]](#cite_note-207)

### Societies and clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Societies and clubs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Ladd_Observatory2.jpg/250px-Ladd_Observatory2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ladd_Observatory2.jpg)

[Ladd Observatory](/wiki/Ladd_Observatory "Ladd Observatory"), built 1890–1891, is used by Brown Space Engineering, a student group focused on [aerospace engineering](/wiki/Aerospace_engineering "Aerospace engineering").

The earliest societies at Brown were devoted to oration and debate. The Pronouncing Society is mentioned in the diary of [Solomon Drowne](/wiki/Solomon_Drowne "Solomon Drowne"), class of 1773, who was voted its president in 1771.[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14) The organization seems to have disappeared during the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). Subsequent societies include the Misokosmian Society (est. 1798 and renamed the Philermenian Society), the Philandrian Society (est. 1799), the United Brothers (1806), the Philophysian Society (1818), and the Franklin Society (1824). Societies served social as well as academic purposes, with many supporting literary debate and amassing large libraries.[[198]](#cite_note-208)[[199]](#cite_note-brown.edu-209) Older societies generally aligned with [Federalists](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party") while younger societies generally leaned [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

Societies remained popular into the 1860s, after which they were largely replaced by fraternities.[[199]](#cite_note-brown.edu-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Liber_Brunensis_%281910%29-Cammarian-Club-without-names.jpg/250px-Liber_Brunensis_%281910%29-Cammarian-Club-without-names.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liber_Brunensis_(1910)-Cammarian-Club-without-names.jpg)

The Cammarian Club in 1910

The Cammarian Club was at first a semi-secret society that "tapped" 15 seniors each year. In 1915, self-perpetuating membership gave way to popular election by the student body, and thenceforward the club served as the *de facto* undergraduate student government. The organization was dissolved in 1971 and ultimately succeeded by a formal student government.

[Societas Domi Pacificae](/wiki/Societas_Domi_Pacificae "Societas Domi Pacificae"), known colloquially as "Pacifica House", is a present-day, self-described secret society. It purports a continuous line of descent from the Franklin Society of 1824, citing a supposed intermediary "Franklin Society" traceable in the nineteenth century.

### LGBTQ+

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: LGBTQ+")]

In 2023, 38% of Brown's students identified as being LGBTQ+, in a poll by *[The Brown Daily Herald](/wiki/The_Brown_Daily_Herald "The Brown Daily Herald")*.[[200]](#cite_note-BrownLGBT23-210) The 2023 LGBTQ+ self-identification level was an increase, up from 14% LGBT identification in 2010.[[200]](#cite_note-BrownLGBT23-210) "Bisexual" was the most common answer amongst LGBTQ+ respondents to the poll.[[200]](#cite_note-BrownLGBT23-210)

### Resource centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Resource centers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Sarah_Doyle_Womens_Center_Brown_University.jpg/250px-Sarah_Doyle_Womens_Center_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sarah_Doyle_Womens_Center_Brown_University.jpg)

The [Sarah Doyle Women's Center](/wiki/Sarah_Doyle_Women%27s_Center "Sarah Doyle Women's Center")

Brown has several resource centers on campus. The centers often act as sources of support as well as safe spaces for students to explore certain aspects of their identity. Additionally, the centers often provide physical spaces for students to study and have meetings. Although most centers are identity-focused, some provide academic support as well.

The [Brown Center for Students of Color](/wiki/Brown_Center_for_Students_of_Color "Brown Center for Students of Color") (BCSC) is a space that provides support for students [of color](/wiki/People_of_color "People of color"). Established in 1972 at the demand of student protests, the BCSC encourages students to engage in critical dialogue, develop leadership skills, and promote [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice").[[201]](#cite_note-211) The center houses various programs for students to share their knowledge and engage in discussion. Programs include the Third World Transition Program, the Minority Peer Counselor Program, the Heritage Series, and other student-led initiatives. Additionally, the BCSC hopes to foster community among the students it serves by providing spaces for students to meet and study.

The [Sarah Doyle Women's Center](/wiki/Sarah_Doyle_Women%27s_Center "Sarah Doyle Women's Center") aims to provide a space for members of the Brown community to examine and explore issues surrounding [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender").[[202]](#cite_note-212) The center was named after one of the first women to attend Brown, [Sarah Doyle](/wiki/Sarah_Doyle "Sarah Doyle"). The center emphasizes [intersectionality](/wiki/Intersectionality "Intersectionality") in its conversations on gender, encouraging people to see gender as present and relevant in various aspects of life. The center hosts programs and workshops in order to facilitate dialogue and provide resources for students, faculty, and staff.[[203]](#cite_note-213)

Other centers include the LGBTQ Center, the Undocumented, First-Generation College and Low-Income Student (U-FLi) Center,[[204]](#cite_note-214) and the Curricular Resource Center.

### Activism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Activism")]

#### 1968 Black Student Walkout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: 1968 Black Student Walkout")]

On December 5, 1968, several Black women from Pembroke College initiated a walkout in protest of an atmosphere at the colleges described by Black students as a "stifling, frustrating, [and] degrading place for Black students" after feeling the colleges were non-responsive to their concerns. In total, 65 Black students participated in the walkout. Their principal demand was to increase Black student enrollment to 11% of the student populace, in an attempt to match that of the proportion in the US. This ultimately resulted in a 300% increase in Black enrollment the following year, but some demands have yet to be met.[[205]](#cite_note-215)[[206]](#cite_note-216)

#### Rape lists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Rape lists")]

In the 1990s, walls in women's bathrooms in the John D. Rockefeller Jr. Library were used to graffiti names of alleged male rapists.[[207]](#cite_note-217) The practice caused the school to re-evaluate how it handles sexual misbehaviour on campus.[[208]](#cite_note-218)[[209]](#cite_note-219)

#### Divestment from South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Divestment from South Africa")]

In the mid-1980s, under student pressure, the university divested from certain companies involved in South Africa. Some students were still unsatisfied with partial divestment and began a fast in Manning Chapel and the university disenrolled them. In April 1987, "dozens" of students interrupted a university corporation meeting, leading to 20 being put on probation.[[210]](#cite_note-BAM-220)

#### Protest of speech by NYPD Commissioner Ray Kelly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Protest of speech by NYPD Commissioner Ray Kelly")]

In 2013, students and Providence community members [protested and disrupted a speech](/wiki/Protest_of_Ray_Kelly_at_Brown_University "Protest of Ray Kelly at Brown University") by then-NYPD Commissioner [Raymond Kelly](/wiki/Raymond_Kelly "Raymond Kelly"). The incident was cited by [Greg Lukianoff](/wiki/Greg_Lukianoff "Greg Lukianoff"), president of the [Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression](/wiki/Foundation_for_Individual_Rights_and_Expression "Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression"), as a high-profile example of a form of student protest questioning conceptions of free speech.[[211]](#cite_note-221) The incident was also the subject of a short critical documentary in 2016 by Brown alumnus Rob Montz.[[212]](#cite_note-222)

#### Israel-Gaza protests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Israel-Gaza protests")]

In early November 2023, twenty students of Jewish background affiliated with the campus group "Jews for Ceasefire Now" (later renamed "Jews for Palestinian Liberation") staged a sit in at University Hall, resulting in their arrests by order of the University administration.[[213]](#cite_note-223) The students were protesting the [Gaza genocide](/wiki/Gaza_genocide "Gaza genocide") and calling for a ceasefire, as well as for the university to divest from companies that "facilitate the 'Israeli military occupation' in Gaza." A few weeks later, the campus was rocked by the shooting of an undergraduate student from Palestine, Hisham Awartani, while he was visiting family in Vermont.[[214]](#cite_note-224) In the wake of the shooting, in early December 2023, forty-one more students held a sit-in with similar demands which resulted in additional arrests by the University. The following semester, as the administration continued to press charges on the forty-one sit-in participants,[[215]](#cite_note-225) nineteen students participated in an eight-day hunger strike preceding a corporation meeting with the demand to present their case to corporation members.[[216]](#cite_note-226) During the wave of [pro-Palestine university protests](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses") in the spring of that year, Brown University students erected a "Gaza Solidarity Encampment" on the University's Main Green, which involved over 80 students and stood for a week before negotiations between the protestors and the Brown administration brought the standoff to a close, resulting in a large number of students being put on probations of various lengths.[[217]](#cite_note-227) In October 2024, the corporation held an early vote on the issue of divestment, rejecting the proposal in an 8-2 vote.[[218]](#cite_note-228) Following a University investigation into student protests resulting from this decision, the University suspended the [Students for Justice in Palestine](/wiki/Students_for_Justice_in_Palestine "Students for Justice in Palestine") chapter at Brown.

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [Brown Bears](/wiki/Brown_Bears "Brown Bears")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/The_1879_Brown_University_Baseball_Team.jpg/250px-The_1879_Brown_University_Baseball_Team.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_1879_Brown_University_Baseball_Team.jpg)

The 1879 Brown baseball varsity, with [W.E. White](/wiki/William_Edward_White "William Edward White") seated second from right. White's appearance in an 1879 major league game may be the first person of color to play professional baseball, 68 years before [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson").[[219]](#cite_note-229)[[220]](#cite_note-230)[[221]](#cite_note-231)[[222]](#cite_note-232)

Brown is a member of the [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") athletic conference, which is categorized as a [Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") (top-level) conference of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA).

The Brown Bears has one of the largest university sports programs in the United States, sponsoring 32 [varsity](/wiki/Varsity_team "Varsity team") intercollegiate teams.[[223]](#cite_note-233) Brown's athletic program is one of the *U.S. News & World Report* top 20—the "College Sports Honor Roll"—based on breadth of the program and athletes' graduation rates.

Athletic facilities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Brown_Stadium_Providence_Rhode_Island_wide_view.jpg/330px-Brown_Stadium_Providence_Rhode_Island_wide_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_Stadium_Providence_Rhode_Island_wide_view.jpg)

[Brown Stadium](/wiki/Brown_Stadium "Brown Stadium") (opened 1925)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Nelson_Fitness_Center_%28Brown%29.jpg/250px-Nelson_Fitness_Center_%28Brown%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nelson_Fitness_Center_(Brown).jpg)

Nelson Fitness Center (opened 2012)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Brown_University%27s_Marston_Boathouse.jpg/250px-Brown_University%27s_Marston_Boathouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brown_University%27s_Marston_Boathouse.jpg)

Marston Boathouse, on the [Seekonk River](/wiki/Seekonk_River "Seekonk River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Meehan_Auditorium_Brown_University.jpg/330px-Meehan_Auditorium_Brown_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meehan_Auditorium_Brown_University.jpg)

[Meehan Auditorium](/wiki/Meehan_Auditorium "Meehan Auditorium") (opened 1961)

Brown's newest varsity team is women's rugby, promoted from club-sport status in 2014. Brown women's rowing has won 7 national titles between 1999 and 2011.[[224]](#cite_note-234) [Brown men's rowing](/wiki/Brown_University_Rowing "Brown University Rowing") perennially finishes in the top 5 in the nation, most recently winning silver, bronze, and silver in the national championship races of 2012, 2013, and 2014. The men's and women's crews have also won championship trophies at the [Henley Royal Regatta](/wiki/Henley_Royal_Regatta "Henley Royal Regatta") and the [Henley Women's Regatta](/wiki/Henley_Women%27s_Regatta "Henley Women's Regatta"). Brown's men's soccer is consistently ranked in the top 20[[225]](#cite_note-235) and has won 18 Ivy League titles overall; recent[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] soccer graduates play professionally in [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") and overseas.

Brown football, under its most successful coach historically, [Phil Estes](/wiki/Phil_Estes "Phil Estes"), won Ivy League championships in 1999, 2005, and 2008. high-profile alumni of the football program include former [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans") head coach [Bill O'Brien](/wiki/Bill_O%27Brien_(American_football) "Bill O'Brien (American football)"); former [Penn State](/wiki/Pennsylvania_State_University "Pennsylvania State University") football coach [Joe Paterno](/wiki/Joe_Paterno "Joe Paterno"), [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") namesake [John W. Heisman](/wiki/John_W._Heisman "John W. Heisman"), and Pollard Award namesake [Fritz Pollard](/wiki/Fritz_Pollard "Fritz Pollard").

Brown women's gymnastics won the Ivy League tournament in 2013 and 2014. The Brown women's sailing team has won 5 national championships, most recently in 2019[[226]](#cite_note-236) while the coed sailing team won 2 national championships in 1942 and 1948.[[227]](#cite_note-237) Both teams are consistency ranked in the top 10 in the nation.[[228]](#cite_note-238)

The first intercollegiate ice hockey game in America was played between Brown and Harvard on January 19, 1898.[[229]](#cite_note-239) The first university rowing regatta larger than a dual-meet was held between Brown, Harvard, and Yale at [Lake Quinsigamond](/wiki/Lake_Quinsigamond "Lake Quinsigamond") in Massachusetts on July 26, 1859.[[230]](#cite_note-240)[[13]](#cite_note-:7-14)

Brown also supports competitive intercollegiate club sports, including [ultimate frisbee](/wiki/Ultimate_(sport) "Ultimate (sport)"). The men's ultimate team, Brownian Motion, has won four national championships, in 2000, 2005, 2019 and 2024.[[231]](#cite_note-241)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Notable people")]

### Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Alumni")]

Main article: [List of Brown University alumni](/wiki/List_of_Brown_University_alumni "List of Brown University alumni")

Alumni in politics and government include [U.S. Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") [John Hay](/wiki/John_Hay "John Hay") (1852), U.S. Secretary of State and [U.S. Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") [Richard Olney](/wiki/Richard_Olney "Richard Olney") (1856), [Chief Justice of the United States](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") and U.S. Secretary of State [Charles Evans Hughes](/wiki/Charles_Evans_Hughes "Charles Evans Hughes") (1881), [Governor of Wyoming Territory](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Wyoming "List of governors of Wyoming") and [Nebraska Governor](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Nebraska "List of governors of Nebraska") [John Milton Thayer](/wiki/John_Milton_Thayer "John Milton Thayer") (1841), [Rhode Island Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Rhode_Island "Governor of Rhode Island") [Augustus Bourn](/wiki/Augustus_O._Bourn "Augustus O. Bourn") (1855), [Louisiana Governor](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Louisiana "List of governors of Louisiana") [Bobby Jindal](/wiki/Bobby_Jindal "Bobby Jindal") '92, [U.S. Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") [Maggie Hassan](/wiki/Maggie_Hassan "Maggie Hassan") '80 of New Hampshire, [Delaware Governor](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Delaware "List of governors of Delaware") [Jack Markell](/wiki/Jack_Markell "Jack Markell") '82, Rhode Island Representative [David Cicilline](/wiki/David_Cicilline "David Cicilline") '83, Minnesota Representative [Dean Phillips](/wiki/Dean_Phillips "Dean Phillips") '91, [2020](/wiki/2020_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries") Presidential candidate and entrepreneur [Andrew Yang](/wiki/Andrew_Yang "Andrew Yang") '96, [DNC Chair](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee") [Tom Perez](/wiki/Tom_Perez "Tom Perez") '83, diplomat [Richard Holbrooke](/wiki/Richard_Holbrooke "Richard Holbrooke") '62, and career United States diplomat [W. Stuart Symington](/wiki/W._Stuart_Symington_(diplomat) "W. Stuart Symington (diplomat)") '74.[[232]](#cite_note-242)

Prominent alumni in business and finance include philanthropist [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Watson Jr.") '37, co-founder of [Starwood Capital Group](/wiki/Starwood_Capital_Group "Starwood Capital Group") [Barry Sternlicht](/wiki/Barry_Sternlicht "Barry Sternlicht") '82, [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") CEO [John Sculley](/wiki/John_Sculley "John Sculley") '61, [Blackberry Ltd.](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited") CEO [John S. Chen](/wiki/John_S._Chen "John S. Important figures in the history of education include the father of American public school education [Horace Mann](/wiki/Horace_Mann "Horace Mann") (1819), civil [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") and [Amherst College](/wiki/Amherst_College "Amherst College") president [Alexander Meiklejohn](/wiki/Alexander_Meiklejohn "Alexander Meiklejohn"), first president of the [University of South Carolina](/wiki/University_of_South_Carolina "University of South Carolina") [Jonathan Maxcy](/wiki/Jonathan_Maxcy "Jonathan Maxcy") (1787), [Bates College](/wiki/Bates_College "Bates College") founder [Oren B. Cheney](/wiki/Oren_B._Cheney "Oren B. Cheney") (1836), [University of Michigan](/wiki/University_of_Michigan "University of Michigan") president (1871–1909) [James Burrill Angell](/wiki/James_Burrill_Angell "James Burrill Angell") (1849), [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") president (1899–1919) [Benjamin Ide Wheeler](/wiki/Benjamin_Ide_Wheeler "Benjamin Ide Wheeler") (1875), and [Morehouse College](/wiki/Morehouse_College "Morehouse College")'s first African-American president [John Hope](/wiki/John_Hope_(educator) "John Hope (educator)") (1894).

Alumni in the computer sciences and industry include architect of [Intel 386](/wiki/Intel_80386 "Intel 80386"), [486](/wiki/Intel_80486 "Intel 80486"), and [Pentium](/wiki/Pentium "Pentium") microprocessors [John H. Crawford](/wiki/John_H._Crawford "John H. Crawford") '75, inventor of the first silicon transistor [Gordon Kidd Teal](/wiki/Gordon_Kidd_Teal "Gordon Kidd Teal") '31, [MongoDB](/wiki/MongoDB "MongoDB") founder [Eliot Horowitz](/wiki/Eliot_Horowitz "Eliot Horowitz") '03, [Figma](/wiki/Figma_(software) "Figma (software)") founder [Dylan Field](/wiki/Dylan_Field "Dylan Field"), and Macintosh developer [Andy Hertzfeld](/wiki/Andy_Hertzfeld "Andy Hertzfeld") '75.

Other notable alumni include "Lafayette of the [Greek Revolution](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence")" and its historian [Samuel Gridley Howe](/wiki/Samuel_Gridley_Howe "Samuel Gridley Howe") (1821), [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") head during first seven [Apollo](/wiki/Apollo_program "Apollo program") missions [Thomas O. Paine](/wiki/Thomas_O._Paine "Thomas O. Paine") '42, sportscaster [Chris Berman](/wiki/Chris_Berman "Chris Berman") '77, [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans") head coach [Bill O'Brien](/wiki/Bill_O%27Brien_(American_football) "Bill O'Brien (American football)") '92, 2018 Miss America [Cara Mund](/wiki/Cara_Mund "Cara Mund") '16, [Penn State](/wiki/Pennsylvania_State_University "Pennsylvania State University") football coach [Joe Paterno](/wiki/Joe_Paterno "Joe Paterno") '50, [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy") namesake [John W. Heisman](/wiki/John_W._Heisman "John W. Heisman") '91, distinguished professor of law [Cortney Lollar](/wiki/Cortney_Lollar "Cortney Lollar") '97, Former SEC Commissioner [Annette Nazareth](/wiki/Annette_Nazareth "Annette Nazareth") '78, Olympic and world champion triathlete [Joanna Zeiger](/wiki/Joanna_Zeiger "Joanna Zeiger"), [royals](/wiki/Royal_family "Royal family") and [nobles](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") such as [Prince Rahim Aga Khan](/wiki/Prince_Rahim_Aga_Khan "Prince Rahim Aga Khan"), [Prince Faisal bin Al Hussein](/wiki/Prince_Faisal_bin_Al_Hussein "Prince Faisal bin Al Hussein") of the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan, Princess [Leila Pahlavi](/wiki/Leila_Pahlavi "Leila Pahlavi") of Iran, [Prince Nikolaos of Greece and Denmark](/wiki/Prince_Nikolaos_of_Greece_and_Denmark "Prince Nikolaos of Greece and Denmark"), [Prince Nikita Romanov](/wiki/Prince_Nikita_Romanov "Prince Nikita Romanov"), [Princess Theodora of Greece and Denmark](/wiki/Princess_Theodora_of_Greece_and_Denmark_(born_1983) "Princess Theodora of Greece and Denmark (born 1983)"), [Prince Jaime of Bourbon-Parma, Duke of San Jaime and Count of Bardi](/wiki/Prince_Jaime,_Count_of_Bardi "Prince Jaime, Count of Bardi"), [Prince Ra'ad bin Zeid](/wiki/Prince_Ra%27ad_bin_Zeid "Prince Ra'ad bin Zeid"), [Lady Gabriella Windsor](/wiki/Lady_Gabriella_Windsor "Lady Gabriella Windsor"), [Prince Alexander von Fürstenberg](/wiki/Prince_Alexander_von_F%C3%BCrstenberg "Prince Alexander von Fürstenberg"), [Countess Cosima von Bülow Pavoncelli](/wiki/Cosima_von_B%C3%BClow_Pavoncelli "Cosima von Bülow Pavoncelli"), and her half-brother [Prince Alexander-Georg von Auersperg](/wiki/Prince_Alexander-Georg_von_Auersperg "Prince Alexander-Georg von Auersperg").

[Nobel](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") Laureate alumni include humanitarian [Jerry White](/wiki/Jerry_White_(activist) "Jerry White (activist)") '87 ([Peace](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), 1997), biologist [Craig Mello](/wiki/Craig_Mello "Craig Mello") '82 ([Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"), 2006), economist [Guido Imbens](/wiki/Guido_Imbens "Guido Imbens") (AM '89, PhD '91; [Economic Sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"), 2021), and economist [Douglas Diamond](/wiki/Douglas_Diamond "Douglas Diamond") '75 ([Economic Sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"), 2022).

### Faculty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Faculty")]

Main article: [List of Brown University faculty](/wiki/List_of_Brown_University_faculty "List of Brown University faculty")

Among Brown's past and present faculty are eight [Nobel Laureates](/wiki/Nobel_Laureates "Nobel Laureates"): [Lars Onsager](/wiki/Lars_Onsager "Lars Onsager") ([Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), 1968), [Leon Cooper](/wiki/Leon_Cooper "Leon Cooper") ([Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), 1972), [George Snell](/wiki/George_Davis_Snell "George Davis Snell") ([Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"), 1980), [George Stigler](/wiki/George_Stigler "George Stigler") ([Economic Sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"), 1982), [Henry David Abraham](/wiki/Henry_David_Abraham "Henry David Abraham") ([Peace](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), 1985), [Vernon L. Smith](/wiki/Vernon_L._Smith "Vernon L. Smith") ([Economic Sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"), 2002), and [J. Michael Kosterlitz](/wiki/J._Michael_Kosterlitz "J. Michael Kosterlitz") ([Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), 2016), [Peter Howitt](/wiki/Peter_Howitt_(economist) "Peter Howitt (economist)") ([Economic Sciences](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"), 2025).

Miller") (Sc.B. 1970); computer scientists [Robert Sedgewick](/wiki/Robert_Sedgewick_(computer_scientist) "Robert Sedgewick (computer scientist)") and [Andries van Dam](/wiki/Andries_van_Dam "Andries van Dam"); economists [Hyman Minsky](/wiki/Hyman_Minsky "Hyman Minsky"), [Glenn Loury](/wiki/Glenn_Loury "Glenn Loury"), [George Stigler](/wiki/George_Stigler "George Stigler"), [Mark Blyth](/wiki/Mark_Blyth "Mark Blyth"), and [Emily Oster](/wiki/Emily_Oster "Emily Oster"); historians [Gordon S. Wood](/wiki/Gordon_S._Wood "Gordon S. Wood") and [Joan Wallach Scott](/wiki/Joan_Wallach_Scott "Joan Wallach Scott"); mathematicians [David Gale](/wiki/David_Gale "David Gale"), [David Mumford](/wiki/David_Mumford "David Mumford"), [Mary Cartwright](/wiki/Mary_Cartwright "Mary Cartwright"), and [Solomon Lefschetz](/wiki/Solomon_Lefschetz "Solomon Lefschetz"); physicists [Sylvester James Gates](/wiki/Sylvester_James_Gates "Sylvester James Gates") and [Gerald Guralnik](/wiki/Gerald_Guralnik "Gerald Guralnik"). Faculty in literature include [Chinua Achebe](/wiki/Chinua_Achebe "Chinua Achebe"), [Ama Ata Aidoo](/wiki/Ama_Ata_Aidoo "Ama Ata Aidoo"), [Julio Ortega](https://vivo.brown.edu/display/jortega), and [Carlos Fuentes](/wiki/Carlos_Fuentes "Carlos Fuentes"). Among Brown's faculty and fellows in political science, and public affairs are the former prime minister of Italy and former [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") chief, [Romano Prodi](/wiki/Romano_Prodi "Romano Prodi"); former [president of Brazil](/wiki/President_of_Brazil "President of Brazil"), [Fernando Cardoso](/wiki/Fernando_Cardoso "Fernando Cardoso"); former [president of Chile](/wiki/President_of_Chile "President of Chile"), [Ricardo Lagos](/wiki/Ricardo_Lagos "Ricardo Lagos"); and son of [Soviet Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_the_Soviet_Union "Premier of the Soviet Union") [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"), [Sergei Khrushchev](/wiki/Sergei_Khrushchev "Sergei Khrushchev"). Other faculty include philosopher [Martha Nussbaum](/wiki/Martha_Nussbaum "Martha Nussbaum"), author [Ibram X. Kendi](/wiki/Ibram_X._Kendi "Ibram X. Kendi"), and public health doctor [Ashish Jha](/wiki/Ashish_Jha "Ashish Jha").

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brown_University&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Main article: [Brown University in popular culture](/wiki/Brown_University_in_popular_culture "Brown University in popular culture")

Mentions of Brown in fiction and popular culture include the following.[[241]](#cite_note-251) *[Family Guy](/wiki/Family_Guy "Family Guy")* character [Brian Griffin](/wiki/Brian_Griffin "Brian Griffin") is a Brown alumnus.[[242]](#cite_note-252) *[The O.C.](/wiki/The_O.C. "The O.C.")*'s main character [Seth Cohen](/wiki/Seth_Cohen "Seth Cohen") is denied acceptance to Brown while his girlfriend [Summer Roberts](/wiki/Summer_Roberts "Summer Roberts") is accepted.[[243]](#cite_note-Alvarez-253)
